In flight, pilots maintain spatial orientation through three sensory systems: visual (primary — horizon, instruments), vestibular (inner ear — motion and gravity), and proprioceptive (body position sensors in muscles and joints).
exam_tip: Spatial orientation requires integration of visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive inputs. Vision is the dominant and most reliable sense for orientation.
key_facts: Awareness of position and movement relative to earth
Three systems: visual, vestibular, proprioceptive
Vision is the primary and most reliable orientation sense

syllabus_desc: Define disorientation.
concept: Disorientation is the inability to correctly determine one's position, attitude, or motion relative to the earth. In aviation, spatial disorientation occurs when the brain receives conflicting or insufficient sensory information, most commonly when visual references are lost (cloud, night, poor visibility). Disorientation is a leading cause of fatal GA accidents.
exam_tip: Disorientation occurs when visual references are lost and the pilot relies on unreliable vestibular senses. Trust instruments, not body sensations.
key_facts: Inability to correctly determine position or attitude
Occurs when visual references are lost
Leading cause of fatal GA accidents

syllabus_desc: Outline the basic anatomy of the motion, orientation and gravitational sensory organs, including: The semi-circular canals
concept: The semi-circular canals are three fluid-filled tubes oriented at right angles to each other, detecting angular (rotational) acceleration in pitch, roll, and yaw. When the head rotates, fluid (endolymph) lags behind due to inertia, deflecting hair cells (cupula) and sending nerve signals to the brain. After approximately 20 seconds of constant rotation, the fluid catches up and the sensation of turning ceases — this is why prolonged turns are dangerous without visual reference.
exam_tip: Semi-circular canals detect angular acceleration only — not constant speed rotation. After ~20 seconds of constant turn, the sensation of turning stops. This is the basis for 'the leans.'
key_facts: Three canals at right angles detect pitch, roll, and yaw rotation
Fluid (endolymph) deflects hair cells during rotation
After ~20 sec of constant rotation, sensation ceases — dangerous without visual reference

syllabus_desc: Outline the basic anatomy of the motion, orientation and gravitational sensory organs, including: Vestibular sac/tubes
concept: The vestibular sacs (utricle and saccule) contain otolith organs — small calcium carbonate crystals (otoconia) resting on hair cells. These organs detect linear acceleration and gravity. The utricle primarily detects horizontal acceleration; the saccule detects vertical acceleration. They cannot distinguish between gravity and linear acceleration, which causes illusions like the somatogravic illusion during takeoff.
exam_tip: Otolith organs detect linear acceleration and gravity but cannot tell them apart — this is the basis of the somatogravic illusion.
key_facts: Utricle and saccule contain otolith organs
Detect linear acceleration and head position relative to gravity
Cannot distinguish between gravity and linear acceleration

syllabus_desc: Outline the physiology of the motion, orientation and gravitational sensory organs, including: The semi-circular canals
concept: The semi-circular canals function by detecting fluid movement caused by angular acceleration. When rotation begins, endolymph lags behind the canal walls, bending the cupula and stimulating hair cells. This generates a nerve signal indicating rotation. When rotation stops, the fluid continues moving briefly, creating a false sensation of rotation in the opposite direction.
exam_tip: Key physiology: fluid lags at start of rotation (detects turn), catches up during constant rotation (loses detection), continues after stopping (false reverse sensation).
key_facts: Endolymph lags behind at start of rotation — detects turn
Fluid catches up during constant rotation — sensation ceases
Fluid continues after stop — false reverse rotation sensation

syllabus_desc: Outline the physiology of the motion, orientation and gravitational sensory organs, including: Vestibular sac/tubes
concept: The otolith organs function by gravitational and inertial force acting on the otoconia crystals. When the head tilts or accelerates linearly, the dense crystals shift position on the hair cells, generating nerve signals indicating tilt or acceleration. At rest, gravity acting on the otoconia provides a constant reference for head position. The brain integrates these signals with visual and proprioceptive information to determine orientation.
exam_tip: Otoconia shift under gravity or acceleration. The brain needs vision to disambiguate — without visual reference, acceleration and tilt feel identical.
key_facts: Otoconia crystals shift under gravity and linear acceleration
Provide constant head-position reference at rest via gravity
Brain needs vision to distinguish tilt from acceleration

syllabus_desc: Explain the interconnection between the visual and kinasthetic senses in maintaining accurate spatial orientation.
concept: Accurate spatial orientation requires integration of all three sensory systems — visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive. Vision provides the dominant and most reliable reference. When all three agree, orientation is accurate. When they conflict (such as in cloud), the brain defaults to the sense it trusts most — which in untrained individuals is often the unreliable vestibular system.
exam_tip: Vision is dominant and reliable. Vestibular and proprioceptive senses can mislead. Instrument flying training teaches pilots to trust visual (instrument) information over body sensations.
key_facts: All three systems must agree for accurate orientation
Vision is dominant and most reliable
Conflict between senses causes disorientation

syllabus_desc: Explain the body’s limitations in maintaining spatial orientation when vision is adversely affected.
concept: When vision is impaired (cloud, night, poor visibility), the body relies on the vestibular system which is unreliable after approximately 20 seconds of constant motion. The vestibular system cannot detect gradual changes in bank or attitude, leading to false sensations. Without visual reference, pilots cannot maintain straight and level flight — spatial disorientation and loss of control typically follow within minutes.
exam_tip: Without vision, spatial orientation is lost within about 178 seconds (less than 3 minutes) on average. This is why VFR into IMC is a leading killer in GA.
key_facts: Vestibular system unreliable after ~20 seconds of constant motion
Cannot detect gradual attitude changes
Loss of control typically follows within minutes without visual reference

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following spatial illusions: The leans and sub-threshold stimulation
concept: The leans occur when a gradual, sub-threshold roll is not detected by the semi-circular canals. When the pilot notices the bank on instruments and corrects, the correction IS detected, creating a false sensation of banking in the opposite direction. The pilot feels they are banking when they are actually wings-level, creating a strong urge to return to the original (banked) attitude.
exam_tip: The leans are the most common vestibular illusion. Caused by sub-threshold roll followed by detected correction. Trust instruments, not body sensation.
key_facts: Gradual roll below vestibular threshold goes undetected
Correction feels like banking the wrong way
Most common vestibular illusion — trust instruments

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following spatial illusions: Somatogravic illusion
concept: The somatogravic illusion occurs during rapid acceleration (takeoff, go-around) when the otolith organs interpret the acceleration force as a backward tilt, creating a false sensation of nose-up pitch. The pilot may instinctively push the nose down, potentially flying into the ground. The reverse occurs during rapid deceleration, creating a false nose-down sensation.
exam_tip: Somatogravic illusion during takeoff: acceleration feels like pitching up, pilot pushes nose down dangerously. Particularly dangerous at night or in poor visibility.
key_facts: Acceleration creates false pitch-up sensation
Pilot may dangerously push nose down
Deceleration creates false pitch-down sensation

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following spatial illusions: Somatogyral illusion
concept: The somatogyral illusion occurs when a prolonged constant-rate turn is not detected by the semi-circular canals (fluid catches up after ~20 seconds). The pilot perceives they are flying straight. When the turn is stopped, the fluid continues moving, creating a strong false sensation of turning in the opposite direction. The pilot may re-enter the original turn to relieve the sensation (graveyard spiral).
exam_tip: Somatogyral illusion leads to the graveyard spiral — the pilot keeps re-entering the turn to relieve the false sensation. Always trust instruments.
key_facts: Prolonged turn goes undetected after ~20 seconds
Stopping the turn creates false reverse-turn sensation
Can lead to graveyard spiral if pilot follows body sensation

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following spatial illusions: Cross coupled turning (Coriolis effect)
concept: The Coriolis illusion occurs when a pilot makes a head movement (looking down at a chart) during a sustained turn. The head movement stimulates semi-circular canals in a different plane, producing a violent, disorienting tumbling sensation. It is one of the most severe vestibular illusions and can completely incapacitate the pilot with nausea and disorientation.
exam_tip: Coriolis illusion: head movement during a turn = violent tumbling sensation. Avoid head movements during turns, especially in IMC. One of the most dangerous illusions.
key_facts: Head movement during sustained turn triggers the illusion
Produces violent tumbling sensation
One of the most severe vestibular illusions — can incapacitate

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following spatial illusions: Pressure vertigo
concept: Pressure vertigo occurs when unequal pressure changes in the two middle ears (e.g., one Eustachian tube blocked) cause asymmetric vestibular stimulation. This creates a sensation of spinning or tilting. It is most common during descent when one ear fails to equalise while the other does. Can cause severe disorientation and nausea.
exam_tip: Pressure vertigo = unequal ear pressure during altitude changes. Associated with blocked Eustachian tube on one side. Another reason not to fly with a cold.
key_facts: Unequal middle ear pressure causes asymmetric vestibular stimulation
Most common during descent with one blocked Eustachian tube
Creates spinning or tilting sensation

syllabus_desc: Explain how disorientation can be prevented.
concept: Disorientation prevention requires maintaining visual reference wherever possible — avoiding VFR flight into IMC conditions. In instrument conditions, pilots must trust instruments and ignore body sensations. Avoid head movements during turns (prevents Coriolis). Use autopilot when available in low-visibility conditions. Regular instrument proficiency training builds the habit of trusting instruments over sensations.
exam_tip: Prevention centres on maintaining visual references and trusting instruments. The golden rule: if in doubt about orientation, trust the instruments, not your body.
key_facts: Maintain visual references — avoid VFR into IMC
Trust instruments, not body sensations
Avoid head movements during turns; use autopilot when available

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of positive and negative accelerations on: The circulatory system
concept: Positive G-forces (+Gz) cause blood to pool in the lower body, reducing blood flow to the brain and heart. The heart must work harder to maintain cerebral blood flow. At approximately +3-4G, blood flow to the eyes is reduced, causing peripheral vision loss (grey-out). At higher G-loads, the heart cannot overcome the G-force, and blood flow to the brain fails.
exam_tip: Positive G affects the circulatory system by pooling blood in the lower body. The heart struggles to pump blood to the brain against the increased G-force.
key_facts: +Gz pools blood in lower body
Heart must work harder to maintain cerebral flow
Reduced venous return decreases cardiac output

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of positive and negative accelerations on: Vision
concept: Positive G-forces reduce blood flow to the eyes, causing progressive visual symptoms. Grey-out (loss of peripheral vision) occurs at approximately +3-4G as retinal blood flow decreases. Blackout (complete loss of vision with consciousness preserved) occurs at approximately +4-5G. Vision recovers rapidly once G-load is reduced.
exam_tip: Grey-out at ~4G, blackout at ~5G. These are visual symptoms — consciousness is still preserved at blackout. G-LOC follows if G is maintained.
key_facts: Grey-out (peripheral vision loss) at approximately +3-4G
Blackout (total vision loss, conscious) at approximately +4-5G
Vision recovers rapidly when G reduced

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of positive and negative accelerations on: Consciousness
concept: If positive G-force is maintained beyond blackout, G-induced Loss of Consciousness (G-LOC) occurs as the brain is completely deprived of blood flow. G-LOC typically occurs above +5G. Recovery after G-LOC takes 15-30 seconds of confusion and disorientation. During this time the aircraft is uncontrolled. G-LOC is potentially fatal.
exam_tip: G-LOC occurs above ~5G with 15-30 second recovery period of confusion. The aircraft is uncontrolled during this time — potentially fatal.
key_facts: G-LOC occurs when brain blood flow ceases, typically above +5G
Recovery takes 15-30 seconds of confusion
Aircraft uncontrolled during G-LOC — potentially fatal

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes and symptoms of black-out.
concept: Blackout is caused by sustained positive G-forces reducing blood flow to the eyes and brain. At approximately +4-5G, arterial pressure at eye level falls below the intraocular pressure, collapsing retinal blood vessels and causing complete vision loss. Consciousness is maintained briefly. Symptoms progress from grey-out to tunnel vision to complete blackout.
exam_tip: Blackout = total vision loss with consciousness preserved, at approximately +4-5G. Precedes G-LOC if G is maintained.
key_facts: Caused by +Gz reducing blood flow to eyes
Occurs at approximately +4-5G
Progressive: grey-out → tunnel vision → blackout

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes and symptoms of red-out.
concept: Red-out is caused by negative G-forces (-Gz, such as during an outside loop or steep pushover) forcing blood into the head and eyes. Increased blood pressure in the head causes engorgement of retinal blood vessels, producing a red visual field. Severe negative G can cause haemorrhagic stroke. Red-out is less common than blackout in normal flight operations.
exam_tip: Red-out = blood forced into head during negative G. Produces red visual field. Less common than blackout but potentially more dangerous due to stroke risk.
key_facts: Negative G forces blood into the head
Retinal vessel engorgement produces red visual field
Severe cases risk haemorrhagic stroke

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of motion sickness.
concept: Motion sickness is caused by sensory conflict between the visual system, vestibular system, and proprioceptive system. When the brain receives mismatched information (e.g., vestibular system detects motion but eyes see a stationary cockpit interior), it interprets this as potential poisoning and triggers nausea. Turbulence, unusual attitudes, and looking inside the cockpit while manoeuvring are common triggers.
exam_tip: Motion sickness = sensory mismatch between visual and vestibular systems. Most common in student pilots and passengers. Looking outside helps.
key_facts: Caused by sensory conflict between visual and vestibular systems
Brain interprets mismatch as potential poisoning
Common triggers: turbulence, looking inside cockpit during manoeuvres

syllabus_desc: Describe how motion sickness can be prevented.
concept: Motion sickness prevention includes looking outside at the horizon (aligns visual and vestibular input), keeping the head still, maintaining a cool cockpit with fresh air, avoiding heavy meals before flight, flying smoothly, and progressive exposure (most people adapt with repeated exposure). For student pilots, short initial flights with gradual increase help build tolerance.
exam_tip: Key prevention: look outside, keep head still, fresh air, fly smoothly. Most people adapt with repeated exposure.
key_facts: Look outside at the horizon to align visual and vestibular input
Keep head still; maintain cool cockpit with fresh air
Progressive exposure builds tolerance over time

syllabus_desc: Describe how motion sickness can be treated.
concept: If motion sickness develops, the pilot should focus on the horizon, open air vents, avoid head movements, and consider landing if symptoms are severe. Once vomiting begins, the pilot's ability to safely control the aircraft may be compromised. Anti-nausea medication can be used for passengers but most are unsuitable for pilots due to drowsiness side effects.
exam_tip: Treatment: horizon focus, fresh air, minimise head movement. If severe, consider landing. Most anti-nausea medications cause drowsiness — unsuitable for pilots.
key_facts: Focus on horizon and open air vents
Consider landing if symptoms are severe
Anti-nausea medications generally unsuitable for pilots (drowsiness)

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of flight anxiety.
concept: Flight anxiety is a stress response triggered by perceived danger or unfamiliarity in the flying environment. Causes include inexperience, previous negative flying experiences, fear of loss of control, fear of mechanical failure, and environmental factors like turbulence or poor weather. Both pilots and passengers can experience flight anxiety.
exam_tip: Flight anxiety is a normal stress response to perceived danger. It can affect both pilots and passengers. Familiarity and preparation reduce it.
key_facts: Stress response to perceived danger in flight
Caused by inexperience, previous incidents, or fear
Affects both pilots and passengers

syllabus_desc: Recognise the signs of flight anxiety in passengers.
concept: Signs of flight anxiety in passengers include visible tension (gripping armrests, rigid posture), rapid shallow breathing, sweating, pallor, verbal expressions of fear, silence or withdrawal, nausea, and in severe cases panic behaviour. Pilots should monitor passengers and respond calmly with reassurance and information.
exam_tip: Watch for physical signs: rigid posture, sweating, pallor, rapid breathing. Respond with calm reassurance and explanation.
key_facts: Physical signs: tension, sweating, pallor, rapid breathing
Behavioural signs: gripping, silence, verbal fear
Respond with calm reassurance and information

syllabus_desc: Describe how flight anxiety can be prevented.
concept: Flight anxiety prevention includes thorough pre-flight briefing of passengers, maintaining a calm professional demeanour, explaining manoeuvres and sounds, smooth flying technique, progressive exposure for anxious passengers, and creating a comfortable cabin environment. For pilot anxiety, thorough preparation, currency, and proficiency training build confidence.
exam_tip: Prevention: brief passengers, fly smoothly, explain what's happening. For pilot anxiety: preparation and proficiency build confidence.
key_facts: Brief passengers thoroughly before flight
Explain manoeuvres and sounds calmly
Pilot anxiety reduced through preparation and proficiency

syllabus_desc: Describe the term fitness to fly.
concept: Fitness to fly means the pilot is physically, mentally, and emotionally capable of safely operating an aircraft. It encompasses medical fitness (no illness or incapacitating condition), mental fitness (adequate rest, no significant stress), and regulatory fitness (valid medical certificate, not under the influence of drugs or alcohol).
exam_tip: Fitness to fly covers physical, mental, and emotional readiness — not just medical certificate validity.
key_facts: Physical, mental, and emotional capability to fly safely
Includes medical, psychological, and regulatory fitness
Assessed before every flight, not just at medical renewals

syllabus_desc: Explain the responsibilities of pilots towards medical fitness for flight.
concept: Pilots have a legal and moral responsibility to ensure they are medically fit before every flight. This goes beyond holding a valid medical certificate — pilots must self-assess before each flight using tools like the IMSAFE checklist. Pilots must not fly if they have any condition that could impair performance, and must consult their DAME if in doubt.
exam_tip: Fitness to fly is the pilot's responsibility before EVERY flight — not just at medical renewals. Use IMSAFE for self-assessment.
key_facts: Legal responsibility to self-assess before every flight
Valid medical certificate is necessary but not sufficient
Consult DAME if in doubt about any condition

syllabus_desc: Identify symptoms and circumstances that would lead you to consult your aviation medical examiner prior to further flight.
concept: Circumstances requiring consultation with a DAME include: unexplained symptoms (dizziness, fainting, chest pain), surgery or hospitalisation, starting new medication, loss of consciousness, significant illness, head injury, changes in vision or hearing, and any condition that could cause sudden incapacitation. When in doubt, consult your DAME before flying.
exam_tip: When in doubt, consult your DAME. Key triggers: unexplained symptoms, new medication, surgery, loss of consciousness, head injury.
key_facts: Unexplained dizziness, fainting, or chest pain
Surgery, hospitalisation, or new medication
Loss of consciousness or head injury

syllabus_desc: Describe the IMSAFE method of assessing fitness for flight.
concept: IMSAFE is a pre-flight self-assessment checklist: Illness (am I sick?), Medication (am I taking anything that could impair performance?), Stress (am I under significant psychological pressure?), Alcohol (have I consumed alcohol within the required time period?), Fatigue (have I had adequate sleep?), Eating/Emotion (have I eaten properly? Am I emotionally fit?). If any factor is unsatisfactory, the pilot should not fly.
exam_tip: IMSAFE: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Eating/Emotion. Use before every flight. Any 'yes' to a concern means don't fly.
key_facts: I = Illness, M = Medication, S = Stress
A = Alcohol, F = Fatigue, E = Eating/Emotion
Any concern means do not fly

syllabus_desc: Describe the problems associated with pregnancy and flying.
concept: Pregnancy affects flying through increased susceptibility to hypoxia (increased oxygen demand), nausea and motion sickness, reduced G-tolerance, altered circulation, and the effects of decompression or barotrauma on the foetus. Pregnant pilots should consult their DAME and many aviation authorities restrict flying during later stages of pregnancy.
exam_tip: Pregnancy increases hypoxia susceptibility, motion sickness, and reduces G-tolerance. Consult DAME for guidance on flying restrictions.
key_facts: Increased susceptibility to hypoxia (higher O2 demand)
Greater risk of motion sickness and reduced G-tolerance
Consult DAME — restrictions apply in later pregnancy

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Arterial disease
concept: Visual acuity is essential for safe pilot operations. NZ CAR 67.05 requires uncorrected visual acuity of at least 6/9 in each eye, correctable to 6/6. Pilots must be able to read aircraft instruments, detect other aircraft, and maintain situational awareness through visual scan. Presbyopia (age-related loss of near focus) is managed through corrective lenses.
exam_tip: Know the CAR 67.05 visual acuity standards: 6/9 uncorrected, correctable to 6/6. Presbyopia is manageable with appropriate correction.
key_facts: Uncorrected acuity: 6/9 each eye; Correctable to 6/6
Regular eye examinations recommended for pilots
Presbyopia managed with appropriate corrective lenses

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Blood pressure
concept: Colour vision is critical for interpreting instrument displays, runway lighting, and traffic signals. NZ requires colour vision competency — pilots must reliably distinguish red and green. Some colour vision deficiencies may be manageable with a special restriction or operational procedure. Complete colour blindness is disqualifying.
exam_tip: Colour vision is tested as part of medical certification. Some deficiencies allow restricted operations; complete colour blindness disqualifies.
key_facts: Must distinguish red and green reliably
Some deficiencies may allow restricted operations
Complete colour blindness is disqualifying

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Diet
concept: Hearing is essential for two-way radio communication and environmental awareness. NZ requires sufficient hearing to conduct safe radio communications on 1600 Hz frequency speech. Mild to moderate hearing loss may be acceptable with clear communication protocols and possibly hearing aids. Severe hearing loss or complete deafness is disqualifying.
exam_tip: Hearing requirement focuses on ability to conduct radio communications. Hearing protection use is encouraged and does not disqualify.
key_facts: Must hear 1600 Hz speech frequencies for radio
Mild hearing loss may be acceptable
Communication protocols and hearing aids may assist

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Exercise
concept: Cardiovascular fitness is essential as the heart must respond to the increased oxygen demands of flight, especially during high workload or emergency procedures. Conditions such as hypertension, previous heart attack, arrhythmias, or structural heart disease require medical assessment. Regular exercise, blood pressure monitoring, and management of risk factors maintain cardiovascular fitness.
exam_tip: Cardiovascular conditions require DAME assessment. Many conditions are manageable with proper medical control and monitoring.
key_facts: Heart must meet increased oxygen demands in flight
Hypertension, arrhythmias, and prior MI require assessment
Risk factor management maintains cardiovascular fitness

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Obesity
concept: Respiratory fitness is necessary to maintain adequate oxygenation during all flight phases. Conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), or sleep apnoea affect oxygen exchange. Sleep apnoea is of particular concern as it causes intermittent oxygen desaturation and daytime fatigue, both dangerous in aviation. Many respiratory conditions require DAME evaluation and possible flight restrictions.
exam_tip: Respiratory conditions, especially sleep apnoea, require careful assessment. Sleep apnoea causes intermittent hypoxia and fatigue — both hazardous.
key_facts: Asthma, COPD, and sleep apnoea require assessment
Sleep apnoea causes intermittent hypoxia and daytime fatigue
Many respiratory conditions are manageable with medical control

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Smoking
concept: Metabolic fitness relates to conditions like diabetes, thyroid disorders, and obesity. Diabetes must be well-controlled to prevent hypoglycaemia (dangerously low blood sugar causing confusion and disorientation) or hyperglycaemia (high blood sugar impairing cognition). Thyroid disorders affect energy and alertness. Obesity increases cardiovascular strain and may contribute to sleep apnoea.
exam_tip: Diabetes requires excellent control — hypoglycaemia during flight is dangerous. Thyroid disorders and obesity affect fitness indirectly.
key_facts: Diabetes must be well-controlled to prevent hypoglycaemia
Hypoglycaemia causes confusion, disorientation, and loss of control
Thyroid disease and obesity indirectly affect fitness

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Respiratory tract infection/allergies (including colds, sinus, hay fever, influenza, asthma)
concept: Neurological fitness is critical as the nervous system controls decision-making, coordination, and situational awareness. Conditions such as epilepsy, migraines, recurrent syncope (fainting), head injury with loss of consciousness, or neurological degenerative disorders require careful assessment. Even well-controlled epilepsy on medication may be disqualifying due to the risk of sudden seizure in flight.
exam_tip: Neurological conditions are serious in aviation context. Epilepsy may disqualify despite medication. Head injury with LOC requires DAME assessment.
key_facts: Epilepsy may be disqualifying despite medication control
Recurrent syncope (fainting) requires investigation
Head injury with loss of consciousness requires assessment

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Food poisoning and gastroenteritis
concept: Psychological fitness involves mental health, stress management, and cognitive function. Conditions such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, or psychotic disorders require assessment. Antidepressant medications vary in their effects — some are safe for flying, others impair judgment or cause drowsiness. Significant stress, recent bereavement, or relationship problems may temporarily affect fitness.
exam_tip: Mental health conditions require DAME assessment. Many are manageable with appropriate medication. Stress and major life events may temporarily affect fitness.
key_facts: Depression and anxiety require assessment and may be manageable
Some antidepressants are safe for flying; others cause drowsiness
Recent bereavement or stress may temporarily affect fitness

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Neurological factors (including fits/epilepsy, brain injury, fainting, headaches, migraines)
concept: Substance use and medication effects are critical to flying fitness. Alcohol impairs judgment and coordination — the legal limit is zero for pilots before flight and a minimum 8-hour interval after drinking. Recreational drugs affect cognition and judgment. Many prescription medications (sedatives, antihistamines, beta-blockers) impair flying ability. Cannabis and other drugs remain disqualifying. Always consult the DAME about any medication.
exam_tip: Eight-hour rule for alcohol is minimum — 'one bottle, one throttle' is safer. Many common medications impair flying; consult DAME before flying.
key_facts: Eight-hour minimum from alcohol to flight
Recreational drugs disqualifying and detectable in testing
Many medications impair judgment, reaction time, or alertness

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Emotional factors (including depression and anxiety)
concept: Musculoskeletal fitness is required to physically control the aircraft. Conditions such as arthritis, back pain, arm weakness, or joint instability must not prevent precise control inputs or emergency manoeuvres. Injuries recovering with physical therapy often allow return to flying once function is restored. Severe or progressive conditions may require restrictions or disqualification.
exam_tip: Musculoskeletal conditions are assessed based on functional capability to control the aircraft. Recovery from injury often allows return to flying.
key_facts: Must have full range of motion and strength for aircraft control
Arthritis and back pain may be manageable with appropriate treatment
Injuries recovering with PT often allow return to flying

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: Dehydration
concept: Infectious disease considerations include conditions that might cause sudden incapacity (e.g., uncontrolled fever, severe influenza, COVID-19) or conditions contagious and serious in enclosed aircraft environments. Pilots recovering from significant illness should have medical clearance and may need a period of reduced flying to rebuild physical conditioning. Minor colds or minor infections typically do not preclude flying if the pilot is otherwise fit.
exam_tip: Fever, severe illness, or contagious serious conditions prohibit flying. After illness, assess recovery before returning to flying.
key_facts: Fever or severe illness prohibits flying
Contagious serious diseases present safety and health risks
Recovery period may be needed after significant illness

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of alcohol on pilot performance.
concept: Alcohol is a central nervous system depressant that impairs pilot performance progressively. Judgment and decision-making are impaired first (even at low BAC), followed by reduced coordination, slower reaction times, degraded vision, and impaired spatial orientation. Alcohol also reduces tolerance to hypoxia, increases susceptibility to spatial disorientation, and disrupts sleep quality. Effects persist well beyond the period of intoxication — hangover effects can impair performance for 24+ hours.
exam_tip: Judgment is impaired FIRST — this is key because the pilot cannot recognise their own impairment. Effects persist well beyond intoxication (hangover). Alcohol compounds hypoxia risk.
key_facts: Judgment and decision-making impaired first
Reduces hypoxia tolerance — each 1,000 ft altitude adds effect of one drink
Hangover effects persist 24+ hours beyond intoxication

syllabus_desc: Explain the restriction associated with the consumption of alcohol and flying.
concept: NZ CAR 91.207 requires a minimum 8 hours between consumption of alcohol and flying ('bottle to throttle'). However, 8 hours is a MINIMUM — BAC must also be zero before flying. The 8-hour rule alone may be insufficient after heavy drinking. Pilots must also be free from any after-effects of alcohol (hangover). Some operators and airlines impose longer restrictions (e.g., 12 or 24 hours).
exam_tip: Know the NZ rule: 8 hours minimum AND zero BAC AND no after-effects. The 8-hour rule alone is insufficient after heavy drinking.
key_facts: NZ CAR 91.207: minimum 8 hours bottle-to-throttle
BAC must be zero — 8 hours alone may not be enough
Must also be free from after-effects (hangover)

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of drugs on pilot performance.
concept: Drugs (prescription, over-the-counter, and recreational) can impair pilot performance through sedation, reduced reaction time, impaired judgment, visual disturbance, and altered mood. Common aviation-problematic drugs include antihistamines (drowsiness), decongestants (stimulation then rebound fatigue), analgesics (drowsiness, impaired concentration), and anti-anxiety medications (sedation). Even 'non-drowsy' formulations may impair at altitude. All medication use should be discussed with a DAME.
exam_tip: Any medication can potentially impair flying — always consult DAME. Antihistamines are the most common problematic OTC medication for pilots.
key_facts: Prescription and OTC drugs can impair pilot performance
Antihistamines cause drowsiness — most common issue
Always consult DAME before flying on any medication

syllabus_desc: Describe considerations associated with the taking of over the counter medication and flying.
concept: Over-the-counter medications pose particular risks because pilots may assume they are safe. Common issues include: antihistamines (drowsiness, impaired concentration), decongestants (initially stimulating then rebound sedation), analgesics (some contain codeine causing drowsiness), cough suppressants (may cause drowsiness), and sleep aids (prolonged sedation). The underlying condition requiring medication may itself be a flying risk. Pilots should consult their DAME before taking any OTC medication and flying.
exam_tip: The underlying condition is often as much a risk as the medication itself. Don't fly with a cold regardless of medication. Always consult DAME.
key_facts: Antihistamines cause drowsiness even in 'non-drowsy' formulations at altitude
The underlying illness may itself impair flying ability
Consult DAME before flying on any OTC medication

syllabus_desc: Explain why illegal/recreational drugs are unacceptable for pilots.
concept: Illegal and recreational drugs are absolutely prohibited for pilots. Cannabis impairs reaction time, judgment, and memory for 24+ hours after use. Cocaine and stimulants cause unpredictable cardiovascular effects and crash fatigue. Opioids impair consciousness and reaction time. All recreational drugs have unpredictable effects, long detection windows, and no safe dose for aviation. Positive drug tests result in licence revocation.
exam_tip: Zero tolerance for recreational drugs in aviation. Cannabis effects persist 24+ hours. Positive test = licence revocation.
key_facts: Zero tolerance — any use is incompatible with flying
Cannabis impairs for 24+ hours after use
Positive drug test results in licence revocation

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on the body of donating blood.
concept: Donating whole blood (approximately 450-500 mL) temporarily reduces the body's oxygen-carrying capacity by removing haemoglobin-containing red blood cells. This is equivalent to a form of anaemic hypoxia. The body compensates by increasing heart rate and redistributing blood flow, but full red blood cell replacement takes 4-8 weeks. In the interim, reduced oxygen-carrying capacity makes the pilot more susceptible to the effects of altitude.
exam_tip: Blood donation = temporary anaemic hypoxia. The pilot is more susceptible to altitude effects until blood volume is fully restored.
key_facts: Removes ~500 mL blood, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity
Creates temporary anaemic hypoxia
Full red blood cell recovery takes 4-8 weeks

syllabus_desc: State the recommended time period between the donation of blood and flying.
concept: The recommended minimum waiting period is 48 hours after donating whole blood and 24 hours after donating plasma or platelets before flying. These are minimum periods — individual recovery varies and pilots should assess their fitness using IMSAFE before flying after any donation. Some authorities recommend longer periods for operations at higher altitudes.
exam_tip: 48 hours after whole blood, 24 hours after plasma. These are minimums — use IMSAFE to self-assess.
key_facts: 48 hours minimum after whole blood donation
24 hours minimum after plasma or platelet donation
These are minimums — individual recovery varies

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: Carbon monoxide
concept: Carbon monoxide (CO) binds to haemoglobin 200-250 times more readily than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin and reducing oxygen-carrying capacity (anaemic hypoxia). Symptoms progress from headache and dizziness to nausea, confusion, cherry-red lips/skin, loss of consciousness, and death. Treatment: immediately open all vents and windows, turn off cabin heater, use supplemental oxygen if available, and land as soon as possible. CO exposure at altitude compounds hypoxic effects.
exam_tip: CO binds 200-250x more readily than O2. Cherry-red lips are a classic sign. Treatment: fresh air + O2 + land ASAP.
key_facts: CO binds haemoglobin 200-250x more readily than oxygen
Symptoms: headache, nausea, cherry-red lips, confusion
Treatment: ventilate cockpit, O2 if available, land ASAP

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: Fuel
concept: Aviation fuel (avgas, Jet A-1) causes irritation to skin, eyes, and mucous membranes. Prolonged skin contact causes dermatitis. Inhalation of fuel vapour causes headache, dizziness, nausea, and respiratory irritation. High concentrations can cause narcosis and unconsciousness. Treatment: move to fresh air, wash affected skin with soap and water, flush eyes with clean water, and seek medical attention if symptoms persist.
exam_tip: Fuel is a skin and respiratory irritant. Treatment: fresh air and thorough washing. Avoid prolonged skin contact during refuelling.
key_facts: Skin irritant causing dermatitis with prolonged contact
Vapour inhalation causes headache, dizziness, nausea
Treatment: fresh air, wash skin, flush eyes

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: Lubricating oils
concept: Aviation lubricating oils can cause skin irritation and dermatitis with prolonged or repeated contact. Inhalation of oil mist or fumes (often noticed as a peculiar smell in the cockpit) causes respiratory irritation, headache, and nausea. Hot oil contact causes burns. Treatment: remove contaminated clothing, wash affected skin thoroughly, ventilate cockpit if oil fumes detected, and seek medical attention for burns or persistent symptoms.
exam_tip: Oil fumes in the cockpit indicate a potential engine or system leak — treat as both a health hazard and a mechanical concern.
key_facts: Causes skin irritation and dermatitis
Oil fumes indicate potential engine/system leak
Treatment: wash skin, ventilate, seek medical attention

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: Hydraulic fluids
concept: Hydraulic fluids (such as Skydrol) are highly irritating to skin, eyes, and respiratory tract. Contact causes chemical burns to skin and severe eye damage. Inhalation of mist or vapour causes respiratory distress. Some hydraulic fluids are toxic if ingested. Treatment: immediately flush affected skin and eyes with large amounts of water for at least 15 minutes, remove contaminated clothing, ventilate cockpit, and seek medical attention urgently.
exam_tip: Hydraulic fluids are MORE hazardous than fuel or oil — they cause chemical burns. Immediate flushing with water is critical.
key_facts: Highly irritating — causes chemical burns to skin and eyes
More hazardous than fuel or oil
Treatment: flush with water for 15+ minutes, seek urgent medical attention

syllabus_desc: State the source of carbon monoxide poisoning in general aviation aircraft.
concept: In general aviation aircraft, the primary source of carbon monoxide is exhaust gases leaking into the cockpit through defects in the exhaust system or cabin heater. Most GA cabin heaters work by passing air over the exhaust manifold — any crack or corrosion in the manifold allows CO to enter the heated air supply. This makes CO poisoning most likely when the cabin heater is in use.
exam_tip: Cabin heater is the primary CO entry point in GA aircraft — it passes air over the exhaust manifold. CO risk is highest with heater ON.
key_facts: Exhaust gas leaks through defective cabin heater system
Heater passes air over exhaust manifold
Most likely when cabin heater is in use

syllabus_desc: Describe reliable methods for the detection of carbon monoxide.
concept: Reliable CO detection methods include chemical spot detectors (cards that change colour in the presence of CO — inexpensive, should be mounted near pilot's face), electronic CO detectors (provide audible alarm and digital readout), and awareness of symptoms (headache, nausea during heater use). Chemical detectors should be replaced regularly as they have a limited lifespan. Electronic detectors are more reliable but more expensive.
exam_tip: Chemical spot detector cards are the minimum standard for GA — mount near the pilot's face and replace regularly.
key_facts: Chemical spot detector cards change colour (inexpensive, replace regularly)
Electronic CO detectors provide audible alarm
Mount detector near pilot's face for earliest warning

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of eliminating carbon monoxide from the cockpit.
concept: To eliminate CO from the cockpit: immediately turn off the cabin heater (primary source), open all air vents and windows to maximise fresh air ventilation, use supplemental oxygen if available (displaces CO from haemoglobin), and land as soon as practicable. After landing, have the exhaust system and heater inspected before further flight. CO has a half-life of 4-6 hours in the body breathing normal air, reduced to 1 hour with 100% oxygen.
exam_tip: Heater OFF, vents OPEN, O2 on if available, LAND. Have exhaust system inspected before next flight.
key_facts: Turn off cabin heater immediately
Open all vents and windows for maximum ventilation
CO half-life: 4-6 hours on air, ~1 hour on 100% O2

syllabus_desc: Define stress.
concept: Stress is the body's physiological and psychological response to demands or perceived threats. It involves activation of the autonomic nervous system (fight-or-flight response) and release of stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline). Moderate stress enhances performance; excessive stress degrades it. Stress can be caused by environmental factors (noise, heat), physiological factors (fatigue, illness), or psychological factors (workload, personal problems).
exam_tip: Stress is a RESPONSE to demands, not the demands themselves. Moderate stress helps; excessive stress harms. Know the Yerkes-Dodson curve.
key_facts: Stress is the body's response to demands or threats
Activates fight-or-flight and stress hormones
Moderate stress enhances performance; excessive stress degrades it

syllabus_desc: Describe a simple model of stress.
concept: The Yerkes-Dodson Law describes an inverted-U relationship between arousal (stress) and performance. At low arousal, performance is poor due to lack of motivation and attention. At moderate arousal, performance peaks — the pilot is alert, focused, and responsive. At high arousal (excessive stress), performance declines rapidly due to anxiety, tunnel vision, and cognitive overload. The optimum arousal level varies by task complexity — complex tasks require lower arousal for peak performance.
exam_tip: Yerkes-Dodson = inverted U curve. Low stress = poor performance. Moderate = optimal. High = degraded. Complex tasks need lower arousal.
key_facts: Yerkes-Dodson Law: inverted-U relationship
Performance peaks at moderate arousal/stress
Complex tasks require lower arousal for optimal performance

syllabus_desc: Define arousal.
concept: Arousal is the level of physiological and psychological activation or alertness. It ranges from deep sleep (minimal arousal) through relaxed wakefulness, alert attention, high alertness, to extreme agitation (maximum arousal). In aviation, appropriate arousal ensures pilots are alert and responsive. Arousal is influenced by workload, stress, fatigue, circadian rhythm, and stimulants.
exam_tip: Arousal = level of activation/alertness. Ranges from sleep to extreme agitation. Moderate arousal is optimal for flying.
key_facts: Arousal is the level of physiological and psychological activation
Ranges from deep sleep to extreme agitation
Appropriate arousal ensures pilot alertness and responsiveness

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between stress and arousal.
concept: Stress increases arousal through activation of the sympathetic nervous system. The Yerkes-Dodson Law shows that moderate stress produces optimal arousal and peak performance. Insufficient stress leads to boredom and inattention (under-arousal). Excessive stress causes over-arousal with anxiety, tunnel vision, and reduced cognitive capacity. Pilots must manage their arousal level — recognising when they are under-aroused (boring flight, fatigue) or over-aroused (emergency, conflict).
exam_tip: Stress drives arousal. Under-arousal = boredom/inattention. Over-arousal = anxiety/tunnel vision. Pilots must recognise and manage both states.
key_facts: Stress increases arousal via sympathetic nervous system
Yerkes-Dodson: moderate stress = optimal arousal = peak performance
Both under-arousal and over-arousal impair performance

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: Heat
concept: Physical fitness is the foundation of stress resilience. Regular aerobic exercise (walking, running, cycling, swimming) strengthens the cardiovascular system and improves stress tolerance. Exercise also improves sleep quality and reduces anxiety. Pilots should maintain regular fitness routines as part of professional fitness.
exam_tip: Regular exercise builds stress resilience and improves sleep. Exercise is one of the most effective stress management tools.
key_facts: Aerobic exercise strengthens cardiovascular system
Regular exercise improves stress tolerance
Exercise improves sleep quality and reduces anxiety

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: Cold
concept: Nutrition affects energy levels, mood, and cognitive function. A balanced diet provides steady energy and supports brain function. Skipping meals, excessive caffeine, or sugar crashes reduce resilience to stress. Hydration is critical — even mild dehydration impairs cognition. Pilots should eat regular balanced meals and maintain hydration, especially before challenging flights.
exam_tip: Good nutrition supports cognitive function and resilience. Skipping meals or excessive caffeine increases stress vulnerability.
key_facts: Balanced diet provides steady energy and supports cognition
Hydration is critical for cognitive function
Avoid excessive caffeine and sugar crashes
Eat regular meals, especially before challenging flights

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: Noise
concept: Relaxation techniques reduce acute stress and promote recovery from chronic stress. Progressive muscle relaxation (systematically tensing and releasing muscle groups) reduces physical tension. Breathing exercises (slow, deep breathing from the diaphragm) activate the parasympathetic nervous system, opposing stress. Meditation or mindfulness promotes mental calm. These techniques can be practised regularly or used during stressful situations.
exam_tip: Breathing exercises are quick and effective for acute stress. Slow, deep breathing (4 count in, 6 count out) activates the parasympathetic system.
key_facts: Progressive muscle relaxation reduces physical tension
Slow breathing activates parasympathetic nervous system
Meditation and mindfulness promote mental calm
Practise regularly to build resilience

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: Vibration
concept: Time management and planning reduce the time pressure that creates stress. Planning the flight thoroughly before takeoff (weather, route, fuel, alternates) reduces uncertainty and stress in flight. Scheduling realistic flight times with margins for headwinds or delays prevents the stress of time pressure. Breaking tasks into manageable steps makes large projects less overwhelming.
exam_tip: Thorough pre-flight planning reduces in-flight stress. Realistic schedules with margins prevent time pressure stress.
key_facts: Thorough planning reduces uncertainty and stress
Realistic schedules with margins prevent time pressure
Breaking tasks into steps makes them manageable
Plan for worst-case scenarios

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: Humidity
concept: Social support from colleagues, family, friends, and professional counsellors provides buffering against stress. Talking with others normalises stress and often leads to practical advice or emotional support. Isolation amplifies stress — pilots with social support handle stress better. Professional counselling or employee assistance programs provide expert help when stress becomes unmanageable.
exam_tip: Talk about stress — don't isolate. Colleagues and counsellors provide perspective and support. No shame in seeking help.
key_facts: Social support buffers against stress
Talking normalises stress and provides perspective
Isolation amplifies stress
Professional counselling helps when stress is unmanageable

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of identifying stress.
concept: Stress can be identified through physical signs (increased heart rate, sweating, muscle tension, headaches, digestive problems), behavioural changes (irritability, withdrawal, increased errors, changed eating/sleeping patterns), and cognitive indicators (difficulty concentrating, poor decision-making, negative thinking). Self-awareness and peer monitoring are important — pilots should regularly assess their stress levels using tools like IMSAFE.
exam_tip: Look for physical, behavioural, and cognitive signs. Self-awareness and honest self-assessment are key. IMSAFE covers stress explicitly.
key_facts: Physical: increased heart rate, sweating, tension, headaches
Behavioural: irritability, increased errors, changed habits
Cognitive: poor concentration, bad decisions, negative thinking

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between acute and chronic stress.
concept: Acute stress is a short-term response to a specific event or situation (e.g., engine failure, severe weather encounter). It triggers the fight-or-flight response, enhancing performance temporarily but degrading it if prolonged. Chronic stress develops from ongoing, unresolved pressures over weeks or months (e.g., financial problems, relationship issues, ongoing work pressure). Chronic stress causes sustained cortisol elevation, leading to fatigue, illness, depression, and progressive performance degradation.
exam_tip: Acute = specific event, short-term, may enhance performance briefly. Chronic = ongoing pressure, long-term, always degrades performance and health.
key_facts: Acute: short-term response to specific event, fight-or-flight
Chronic: ongoing unresolved pressure over weeks/months
Chronic stress causes sustained cortisol, fatigue, and illness

syllabus_desc: Describe the physiological effects of stress.
concept: Physiological effects of stress include increased heart rate and blood pressure, rapid shallow breathing (risk of hyperventilation), muscle tension, sweating, dry mouth, digestive disruption, and suppressed immune function. The sympathetic nervous system activates the fight-or-flight response, releasing adrenaline and cortisol. Chronic stress leads to sustained elevation of these hormones, causing cardiovascular disease, weakened immunity, and chronic fatigue.
exam_tip: Stress activates fight-or-flight: heart rate up, breathing fast, muscles tense. Chronic stress leads to real health consequences.
key_facts: Increased heart rate, blood pressure, and breathing rate
Muscle tension, sweating, digestive disruption
Chronic stress weakens immunity and causes cardiovascular disease

syllabus_desc: Describe the psychological effects of stress.
concept: Psychological effects of stress include anxiety, irritability, difficulty concentrating, impaired decision-making, reduced situational awareness, tunnel vision (fixation on one aspect), memory impairment, and emotional instability. Under high stress, pilots may revert to well-practised behaviours even when inappropriate, or may freeze and fail to act. Chronic stress leads to depression, burnout, and withdrawal.
exam_tip: High stress causes tunnel vision and fixation — the pilot focuses on one thing and misses others. This is a major accident factor.
key_facts: Anxiety, irritability, difficulty concentrating
Tunnel vision and fixation under high stress
Chronic stress leads to depression and burnout

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that improve personal stress tolerance.
concept: Fatigue and other human factors are often the root cause of accidents when combined with other stressors. A fatigued pilot with modest weather conditions might make a poor decision that a rested pilot would catch. Fatigue compounds other risks — manage fatigue before it compounds other problems.
exam_tip: Fatigue rarely causes an accident by itself but often contributes to the cascade of errors leading to accidents. Prevent fatigue.
key_facts: Fatigue compounds other risk factors
Often part of accident chain, not sole cause
Prevention is most effective management

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between stress and fatigue.
concept: Stress and fatigue have a bidirectional relationship — stress causes fatigue, and fatigue increases vulnerability to stress. Chronic stress disrupts sleep quality, leading to accumulated fatigue. Fatigued pilots have reduced stress tolerance, making normally manageable situations overwhelming. The combination of stress and fatigue significantly increases error rates and accident risk. Managing both simultaneously requires addressing sleep, workload, and stressor reduction.
exam_tip: Stress causes fatigue AND fatigue worsens stress — a vicious cycle. The combination dramatically increases error rates.
key_facts: Bidirectional: stress causes fatigue, fatigue increases stress vulnerability
Chronic stress disrupts sleep, accumulating fatigue
Combined stress and fatigue dramatically increase accident risk

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of managing stress.
concept: Special considerations for flight instructors include cumulative fatigue from repeated manoeuvres, student-induced stress (fear of student error), and irregular student schedules. Instructors should manage their own fitness, maintain currency through varied flying, and recognize that teaching while fatigued is less effective and less safe.
exam_tip: Flight instructors experience fatigue from repetition and stress. Maintain fitness and recognize that fatigued teaching is ineffective.
key_facts: Flight instructors: cumulative fatigue from repetition
Student-induced stress on top of teaching demands
Fatigued instructors teach less effectively and safely

syllabus_desc: Explain how individuals differ in their requirement for sleep.
concept: Individual sleep requirements vary, but most adults need 7-9 hours of quality sleep per 24-hour period. Some individuals function well on 6 hours; others need 9+. Requirements are genetically influenced and change with age (older adults may need less). Regardless of individual variation, consistently getting less sleep than required accumulates sleep debt, progressively impairing performance. Pilots should know their personal sleep requirement and ensure they meet it before flying.
exam_tip: Most adults need 7-9 hours. Individual variation exists but consistently less than needed accumulates sleep debt. Know YOUR requirement.
key_facts: Most adults need 7-9 hours of quality sleep
Individual requirements are genetically influenced
Sleep debt accumulates progressively, impairing performance

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: Napping
concept: Sleep architecture consists of non-REM (NREM) stages 1-3 and REM (rapid eye movement) sleep. NREM stage 1-2 is light sleep; stage 3 is deep sleep. REM sleep, where dreams occur, is crucial for memory consolidation. A full sleep cycle lasts about 90 minutes. Adults need multiple complete cycles nightly. Disrupted sleep (waking, fragmented sleep) prevents completing full cycles and leaves sleep deprived.
exam_tip: Sleep cycles are 90 minutes. Complete cycles provide restoration. Disrupted sleep is nearly as bad as no sleep.
key_facts: Sleep architecture: NREM 1-3 and REM stages
Full sleep cycle = 90 minutes
Adults need 4-6 complete cycles nightly
Disrupted sleep prevents full cycles

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: Caffeine consumption
concept: Circadian rhythm is the body's internal 24-hour cycle regulating sleep-wake, hormone production (cortisol, melatonin), body temperature, and alertness. The circadian rhythm is set by light exposure — morning light advances the rhythm, evening light delays it. The rhythm controls when the body naturally wants to sleep and wake. Disrupting the rhythm through shift work or travel causes circadian fatigue.
exam_tip: Circadian rhythm is powerful and involuntary. Light exposure shifts it. Jet lag and shift work disrupt it, causing persistent fatigue.
key_facts: Circadian rhythm is internal 24-hour cycle
Controlled by light exposure and schedule
Disruption through shift work or travel causes fatigue
Adaptation takes days

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: Taking sedatives
concept: Sleep hygiene practices promote sleep quality and quantity. Good sleep hygiene includes: consistent sleep schedule (same bedtime and wake time), dark environment, cool temperature (65-68 degrees F), quiet space, avoiding screens 30-60 minutes before bed (blue light suppresses melatonin), avoiding caffeine 4-6 hours before sleep, avoiding alcohol (disrupts sleep architecture), and avoiding heavy meals before bed.
exam_tip: Sleep hygiene is teachable and effective. Consistent schedule is the most important factor. Dark, cool, quiet, no screens before bed.
key_facts: Consistent sleep schedule is most important
Dark, cool (65-68F), quiet environment
Avoid screens, caffeine, alcohol before bed
Avoid heavy meals 2-3 hours before sleep

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: Taking stimulants other than caffeine
concept: Sleep disorders include insomnia (difficulty falling or staying asleep), sleep apnoea (breathing pauses during sleep causing arousal), restless leg syndrome (uncomfortable sensations in legs during sleep), narcolepsy (sudden sleep episodes), and others. Sleep apnoea is particularly dangerous in pilots as it causes intermittent hypoxia and daytime fatigue. Any suspected sleep disorder should be evaluated by a sleep specialist and reported to the DAME.
exam_tip: Sleep apnoea is disqualifying until treated. Daytime sleepiness despite sleeping 'enough' suggests a sleep disorder. Consult sleep specialist and DAME.
key_facts: Insomnia: difficulty sleeping
Sleep apnoea: breathing pauses causing arousal
Sleep disorders cause daytime fatigue and impair performance
Consult sleep specialist and DAME

syllabus_desc: Describe sleep disorders and their effects on pilot performance.
concept: Common sleep disorders affecting pilots include obstructive sleep apnoea (repeated breathing interruptions causing poor sleep quality and daytime sleepiness), insomnia (difficulty falling or staying asleep), and circadian rhythm disorders (jet lag, shift work disorder). Sleep apnoea is particularly dangerous as pilots may not realise their sleep is fragmented. These disorders cause chronic fatigue, impaired concentration, and increased accident risk. Medical assessment and treatment are required.
exam_tip: Sleep apnoea is the most significant sleep disorder for pilots — causes daytime sleepiness without the pilot realising. Requires medical assessment.
key_facts: Sleep apnoea: repeated breathing interruptions, daytime sleepiness
Insomnia: difficulty falling or staying asleep
All sleep disorders require medical assessment and treatment

syllabus_desc: Define fatigue.
concept: Fatigue is a state of reduced mental and physical performance resulting from inadequate sleep, prolonged wakefulness, circadian rhythm disruption, or excessive workload. Fatigue impairs attention, reaction time, judgment, decision-making, and motor coordination. It is one of the most significant human factors threats in aviation. Unlike alcohol, there is no objective test for fatigue — self-assessment using IMSAFE is the primary defence.
exam_tip: Fatigue = reduced performance from inadequate sleep or excessive demand. No objective test exists — self-assessment is critical. One of the biggest HF threats.
key_facts: Reduced mental and physical performance from sleep deficit
Impairs attention, reaction time, judgment, and coordination
No objective test — self-assessment (IMSAFE) is primary defence

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of fatigue and its effect on pilot performance.
concept: Fatigue is caused by insufficient sleep, prolonged wakefulness, circadian rhythm disruption (flying during the circadian low 0200-0600), poor sleep quality, accumulated sleep debt, and excessive physical or mental workload. Effects on pilot performance include slower reaction time, reduced attention and concentration, impaired decision-making, increased error rate, reduced situational awareness, microsleeps, and mood disturbance. Fatigue is a leading factor in aviation accidents.
exam_tip: Key causes: insufficient sleep, circadian disruption, sleep debt. Effects mirror alcohol impairment. Fatigue is a major accident factor.
key_facts: Causes: insufficient sleep, circadian disruption, sleep debt, workload
Effects: slow reactions, poor judgment, increased errors, microsleeps
Circadian low 0200-0600 is the highest-risk period

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of fatigue.
concept: Fatigue symptoms include persistent yawning, heavy eyelids, difficulty concentrating, slower reaction times, reduced motivation, irritability, increased errors, memory lapses, and difficulty maintaining altitude or heading. Advanced fatigue can cause microsleeps — brief involuntary sleep episodes lasting seconds where the pilot is unconscious. The insidious nature of fatigue means pilots may not recognise their own impairment.
exam_tip: Microsleeps are the most dangerous symptom — the pilot is unconscious for seconds without realising. Watch for yawning and difficulty concentrating as early warnings.
key_facts: Yawning, heavy eyelids, difficulty concentrating, slow reactions
Microsleeps: brief involuntary sleep episodes (most dangerous)
Pilots often fail to recognise their own fatigue impairment

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between acute and chronic fatigue.
concept: Acute fatigue results from a single period of extended wakefulness or demanding work. It is reversible with adequate sleep — typically one or two good nights restore performance. Chronic fatigue results from accumulated sleep debt over days or weeks of insufficient sleep. It is NOT reversible by a single night's sleep — recovery requires sustained adequate sleep over several days. Chronic fatigue is more dangerous because the pilot adapts to feeling tired and may not recognise their impairment.
exam_tip: Acute = one bad night, fixable with sleep. Chronic = accumulated over weeks, NOT fixable by one night. Chronic fatigue is more dangerous because the pilot doesn't recognise it.
key_facts: Acute: single period, reversible with 1-2 nights adequate sleep
Chronic: accumulated over days/weeks, requires sustained recovery
Chronic is more dangerous — pilot adapts and doesn't recognise impairment

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on:
concept: Crew Resource Management (CRM) is the effective use of all available human and technical resources to ensure safe flight operations. Resources include crew knowledge and skills, aircraft systems, procedures, communications, and external information (air traffic control, weather). CRM recognises that human error is inevitable but can be managed through systems, procedures, and teamwork.
exam_tip: CRM = using all available resources effectively. Resources are human (crew skills, knowledge), technical (aircraft systems), and procedural (SOPs).
key_facts: CRM uses all available resources for safety
Recognises human error as inevitable and manageable
Requires clear communication and shared mental model

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: The sensitivity and acuity of the sensory system
concept: Leadership in CRM involves the pilot in command (captain) providing clear direction and establishing a safe operating environment. Effective leaders make decisions decisively but invite input, listen to crew concerns, and maintain a culture where crew members feel safe voicing concerns. Poor leadership (authoritarian, dismissive of crew input, making hasty decisions) leads to crews that withhold critical information.
exam_tip: Leadership means listening to crew. Dismissive captains don't hear critical information from crew. Good leaders ask 'What am I missing?'
key_facts: Captain provides clear direction and safe environment
Good leaders listen to crew concerns
Dismissive leadership suppresses critical information

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: Muscular strength
concept: Communication in CRM requires clear, concise information sharing. Pilots must brief crew thoroughly, confirm understanding, speak up if concerned, and listen actively to crew. Common aviation phraseology ensures standardisation. Closed-loop communication (repeat back) confirms understanding. Poor communication (mumbling, unclear instructions, assumptions) leads to misunderstandings and errors.
exam_tip: Clear communication is fundamental. Use standard phraseology, brief thoroughly, listen actively, and confirm understanding.
key_facts: Standard phraseology ensures clarity
Closed-loop communication confirms understanding
Active listening prevents misunderstandings

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: Resilience and reaction times
concept: Teamwork in CRM requires mutual respect and shared commitment to safety. Team members must feel valued and safe voicing concerns. Teamwork includes supporting colleagues, distributing workload, and backing up each other. Cliques, personality conflicts, or hierarchical barriers that prevent information sharing degrade teamwork and safety.
exam_tip: Teamwork requires psychological safety — crew must feel safe speaking up. Respect all team members equally.
key_facts: Teamwork requires mutual respect and psychological safety
Shared commitment to safety overcomes personality differences
Support colleagues and back each other up

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: Sleep/wakefulness patterns
concept: Workload management in CRM involves recognising when workload exceeds capacity and asking for help before errors occur. The captain should monitor crew workload and adjust task distribution. Crew should speak up when workload is high ('I'm getting behind'). Procedures should be designed to manage workload during critical phases (approach, landing).
exam_tip: Speak up early: 'I'm getting behind' prevents errors. Captain monitors crew workload and adjusts accordingly.
key_facts: Recognise when workload exceeds capacity
Ask for help before errors occur
Captain monitors and balances workload

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: Cognitive or mental functioning
concept: Decision-making in CRM involves gathering relevant information, assessing options, consulting the crew, and deciding decisively. The captain makes the final decision but benefits from crew input. Crew should provide information ('The engine temperature is rising') and can question decisions ('Captain, is the landing gear down?'). Shared mental model — crew understanding the captain's intent and likely decisions — supports good decision-making.
exam_tip: Captain decides, but gathers input. Crew can question ('Is the gear down?'). Shared understanding prevents surprises.
key_facts: Captain gathers input before deciding
Crew provides information and can question decisions
Shared mental model supports good decisions

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: The acquisition of new information
concept: Situation awareness in CRM is shared among the crew — all crew members understand aircraft status, flight progress, and potential threats. Shared situation awareness prevents the situation where the captain believes one thing and first officer observes something different. Briefing thoroughly and communicating changes maintains shared awareness.
exam_tip: Situation awareness is shared. Everyone should know the plan and status. Changes should be communicated to maintain shared awareness.
key_facts: Situation awareness is shared among crew
All members understand status and potential threats
Briefing and communication maintain shared awareness

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: The retention and retrieval of stored information in memory
concept: Conflict resolution in CRM addresses disagreements about procedures, decisions, or aircraft status without hierarchical conflict suppressing important information. Phrases like 'I'm concerned about...' or 'Can we reconsider...' allow crew to raise concerns without confrontation. Captains should welcome this kind of input. Unresolved conflict reduces performance and safety.
exam_tip: 'I'm concerned' or 'Can we reconsider' allows crew to raise safety issues. Captain should welcome this input, not shut it down.
key_facts: Address disagreements directly and professionally
Phrases like 'I'm concerned' raise issues without confrontation
Unresolved conflict degrades performance

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: The rate of information processing
concept: Error management in CRM acknowledges that errors occur and focuses on catching them before they cause harm. Procedures are designed with error traps (checklists, cross-checks). Crew should monitor each other for errors and speak up ('I don't see the landing gear down'). A non-punitive reporting culture encourages reporting of errors so they can be learned from.
exam_tip: Checklists and cross-checks trap errors. Monitor each other. Non-punitive reporting culture supports safety learning.
key_facts: Errors are inevitable; procedures trap them
Monitor crew for errors and speak up
Non-punitive reporting culture supports learning

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the normal process of human ageing on: Insight and self-awareness of your individual capabilities
concept: CRM training and proficiency should be ongoing. Airlines and training organisations conduct CRM training covering communication, teamwork, decision-making, and error management. Proficiency checks include not just technical flying but also CRM behaviours. Pilots should seek opportunities to fly with experienced crew, observe good CRM, and build their own CRM skills throughout their career.
exam_tip: CRM is a learnable skill. Training, proficiency checks, and learning from experienced crew all build CRM competence.
key_facts: CRM training is essential
Proficiency includes CRM behaviours
Continuous learning builds CRM skills throughout career

syllabus_desc: Identify the human sensors pilots depend on for information acquisition.
concept: Pilots rely on multiple sensory systems to acquire flight information: vision (eyes), hearing (ears), proprioception (body position and movement), and the vestibular system (inner ear balance). These sensors provide continuous input about aircraft state, environment, and instrument readings. Integration of these sensory inputs is essential for accurate situational awareness and flight control.
exam_tip: Remember the primary human sensors: visual (cockpit instruments, outside view), auditory (radio communications, warnings), proprioceptive (feel of the aircraft), and vestibular (balance and orientation). Vision is the dominant sensor for pilots.
key_facts: Vision is the primary sensor for pilots, accounting for the majority of information acquisition
Hearing provides critical information about radio communications and alert systems
Proprioception and vestibular systems provide feedback about aircraft motion and orientation

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing.
concept: Information processing follows a sequence: sensory input is received through human sensors, attention selects relevant information from competing stimuli, working memory temporarily holds and manipulates this information, decision-making processes occur, and a response is generated. This model explains how pilots perceive, interpret, and act on flight information within the limitations of human cognition.
exam_tip: The basic model is: Sensory Input → Attention → Working Memory → Decision → Response. Bottlenecks can occur at any stage, particularly at the attention and working memory levels.
key_facts: Information processing is sequential and involves multiple cognitive stages
Attention is a limited resource that filters sensory information
Working memory capacity is limited to approximately 7±2 items
Cognitive bottlenecks can reduce processing efficiency

syllabus_desc: Describe short term/working memory.
concept: Short-term or working memory is the cognitive system that temporarily holds and manipulates information during active processing. It has very limited capacity, typically able to retain approximately 7±2 discrete items for only 15-30 seconds without rehearsal. Working memory is essential for immediate task performance such as following ATC clearances or performing mental calculations during flight.
exam_tip: Working memory is severely limited in capacity and duration. Understand that cockpit interruptions, distractions, and high workload can easily cause working memory to lose critical information.
key_facts: Capacity is approximately 7±2 items
Duration is 15-30 seconds without active rehearsal
Highly vulnerable to interference and distraction
Essential for real-time decision-making and task execution

syllabus_desc: Describe long term memory.
concept: Long-term memory is the system that stores information for extended periods, from hours to a lifetime. It has essentially unlimited capacity and serves as the repository for knowledge, skills, procedures, and experiences. Retrieval from long-term memory can vary in speed and accuracy depending on how information was encoded and the presence of retrieval cues. Pilots rely on long-term memory for procedural knowledge and learned skills.
exam_tip: Long-term memory is unlimited but retrieval can fail. Poor encoding (inadequate training) or lack of retrieval cues (missing procedural reminders) can make stored information inaccessible when needed.
key_facts: Essentially unlimited storage capacity
Stores knowledge, skills, procedures, and experiences
Retrieval depends on encoding quality and presence of retrieval cues
Subject to forgetting through decay and interference

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of memory.
concept: Memory has multiple limitations that affect pilot performance. Working memory is severely capacity-limited (7±2 items) and time-limited (15-30 seconds). Long-term memory retrieval can fail due to poor encoding, interference from similar information, or inadequate retrieval cues.
