Memory is also subject to distortion through reconstruction and false memories. Stress, fatigue, and high workload exacerbate these limitations in the cockpit.
exam_tip: Key memory limitations: working memory is small and brief, long-term retrieval can fail, memory distorts over time, and stress worsens all memory functions. This is why checklists and procedures are critical.
key_facts: Working memory is capacity-limited and time-limited
Long-term memory retrieval can fail despite stored information
Memory is reconstructive and prone to distortion
Stress, fatigue, and workload degrade all memory functions

syllabus_desc: Explain mnemonics as a method of retaining and retrieving information from memory.
concept: Mnemonics are memory techniques that create associations or patterns to enhance encoding and retrieval of information. Examples include acronyms (DECIDE model), rhymes, visualizations, and the method of loci. Mnemonics work by linking new information to existing knowledge structures or creating memorable patterns that overcome working memory limitations. In aviation, mnemonics like hazardous attitude antidotes or emergency procedures aid retention and rapid recall under stress.
exam_tip: Mnemonics are powerful tools for overcoming memory limitations. Understand how they create retrieval cues through association and pattern recognition, and why they're more effective than rote memorization.
key_facts: Mnemonics create associations and patterns to enhance memory
Common types include acronyms, visualizations, and rhythmic patterns
Mnemonics work by linking to existing knowledge structures
Particularly valuable for emergency procedures and high-stress recall

syllabus_desc: Explain checklists as a method of retaining and retrieving information from memory.
concept: Checklists externalize memory by providing a written or electronic reference that reduces reliance on fallible human memory. They serve as retrieval cues, preventing omission of critical steps and standardizing procedures across pilots. Checklists are particularly valuable because they compensate for working memory limitations, allow verification of completion, and reduce variability in procedure execution. They are essential tools in modern aviation for safety-critical operations.
exam_tip: Checklists are not memory crutches—they're safety systems that compensate for known human memory limitations. Understand that proper checklist use (item-by-item verification) is more effective than rote memory.
key_facts: Checklists externalize memory and reduce reliance on recall
They provide retrieval cues and prevent omission errors
Checklists standardize procedures across different pilots
Item-by-item verification is more reliable than memory-based procedures

syllabus_desc: Define perception.
concept: Perception is the cognitive process by which sensory information is selected, organized, and interpreted to create meaningful understanding of the environment. Perception is not passive reception of data but an active process influenced by expectations, prior experience, and current mental state. In aviation, perception of instrument readings, visual cues, and radio communications is critical, but perceptual errors can occur when expectations distort interpretation or when critical information is overlooked.
exam_tip: Perception is active interpretation, not passive reception. Understand that expectations and experience shape what pilots perceive, which is why confirmation bias and expectancy errors occur.
key_facts: Perception is active interpretation of sensory data
Perceptual organization involves selective attention and pattern recognition
Perception is influenced by expectations and prior experience
Perceptual errors can lead to misinterpretation of flight information

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of expectation on perception.
concept: Expectation powerfully influences what pilots perceive and how they interpret sensory information. Pilots tend to perceive what they expect to perceive, sometimes overlooking contradictory information. This expectancy effect can lead to confirmation bias (seeking information that confirms expectations) and failure to notice unexpected developments. In high-workload situations, expectation becomes even more dominant, increasing the risk of missing critical cues that don't match expected patterns.
exam_tip: Expectation can blind pilots to unexpected situations. Common scenario: expecting good weather and missing deteriorating conditions, or expecting a specific aircraft and misidentifying an intruder.
key_facts: Pilots perceive what they expect to perceive
Expectation can cause overlooking of contradictory information
Leads to confirmation bias and selective attention
High workload increases reliance on expectations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of experience on perception.
concept: Experience shapes perception by building mental models and pattern recognition capabilities. Experienced pilots perceive flight situations more accurately because they have learned to recognize meaningful patterns and have well-developed expectancies based on extensive exposure. However, experience can also lead to hazardous complacency and over-reliance on habitual patterns, causing experienced pilots to fail to notice novel or atypical situations. The key is maintaining perceptual flexibility and continuing to question expectations.
exam_tip: Experience improves pattern recognition but can cause overconfidence and missed novel situations. Balance expertise with healthy skepticism and explicit verification of assumptions.
key_facts: Experience improves pattern recognition and meaningful perception
Experienced pilots develop accurate mental models of common situations
Experience can lead to complacency and missed novel developments
Perceptual accuracy in experienced pilots depends on continued vigilance

syllabus_desc: Define situational awareness.
concept: Situational awareness (SA) is the pilot's mental model of the current flight situation, including aircraft state, environment, and relevant threats. Endsley's model defines three levels: perception of elements (noticing information), comprehension of their meaning (understanding relationships and implications), and projection of future states (predicting what will happen). Loss of SA is a major factor in aviation accidents. Warning signs include confusion about aircraft state, fixation on one problem, inability to resolve ambiguities, and unresolved discrepancies between instruments or expectations.
exam_tip: Know Endsley's three levels and warning signs of lost SA. Exam commonly asks about recovery strategies and how to recognize when SA is degraded.
key_facts: SA comprises perception, comprehension, and projection
Loss of SA is a significant accident factor
Warning signs: confusion, fixation, ambiguity, unresolved discrepancies
SA is dynamic and must be continuously updated

syllabus_desc: Describe strategies to maintain and enhance situational awareness.
concept: Maintaining SA requires systematic information gathering, continuous cross-checking of instruments and external cues, active questioning of anomalies, and appropriate task prioritization (Aviate-Navigate-Communicate). Key strategies include building and regularly updating mental models, seeking information that challenges assumptions, maintaining adequate workload management, communicating with crew and ATC, and recognizing personal limitations. Regular instrument scans, positional awareness checks, and explicit verification of ambiguous situations enhance SA.
exam_tip: Focus on active strategies: systematic scanning, cross-checking, questioning assumptions, managing workload, and communication. Passive monitoring leads to SA loss.
key_facts: Systematic information gathering and cross-checking are essential
Questioning anomalies and challenging assumptions maintain accuracy
Workload management and prioritization support sustained SA
Communication with crew and ATC enhances shared situational awareness

syllabus_desc: Describe hazardous attitudes.
concept: Hazardous attitudes are dangerous mindsets that impair judgment and decision-making. The five primary hazardous attitudes in aviation are: Anti-authority ('Don't tell me'), Impulsivity ('Do it quickly'), Invulnerability ('It won't happen to me'), Macho ('I can do it'), and Resignation ('What's the use?'). These attitudes bypass normal safety considerations and lead to risk acceptance. Each attitude has a recognizable antidote that pilots can use to counteract the dangerous thinking pattern.
exam_tip: Know all five hazardous attitudes and their antidotes. Exam will likely ask you to identify attitudes from scenario descriptions and select appropriate counteractions.
key_facts: Five hazardous attitudes: Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, Resignation
Each attitude has a specific antidote
Hazardous attitudes are thought patterns that bypass safety
Recognition and counteraction are essential for risk management

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of countering hazardous attitudes.
concept: Each hazardous attitude can be countered with a specific antidote that redirects thinking toward safety. Anti-authority is countered with 'Follow rules, they're usually right.' Impulsivity with 'Not so fast, think first.' Invulnerability with 'It could happen to me.' Macho with 'Taking risks is foolish.' Resignation with 'I'm not helpless.' Pilots counter hazardous attitudes by recognizing when they arise, consciously applying the antidote, and refocusing on safety-based decision-making. Regular self-assessment and crew feedback help identify personal hazard attitude patterns.
exam_tip: Be able to match each attitude with its antidote. Understand that the antidote works by redirecting the underlying thought pattern toward safety reasoning.
key_facts: Anti-authority → 'Follow rules, they're usually right'
Impulsivity → 'Not so fast, think first'
Invulnerability → 'It could happen to me'
Macho → 'Taking risks is foolish'
Resignation → 'I'm not helpless'

syllabus_desc: Describe the error/poor judgement chain.
concept: The error/poor judgement chain describes a sequence of events leading to adverse outcomes: poor judgment leads to a decision or action, which produces consequences, which may be recognized or not, and ultimately results in an accident or incident. The chain highlights that accidents rarely result from a single decision but rather from a series of errors and failures to recognize or correct them. Recognizing and breaking the chain at early stages through error detection and corrective action is essential. Monitoring instruments, questioning inconsistencies, and maintaining SA are key to catching errors before they escalate.
exam_tip: Understand the chain as: Poor judgment → Decision/Action → Consequences → Recognition → Outcome. Breaking the chain early (error detection) is more effective than trying to correct later.
key_facts: Chain: Poor judgment → Decision/Action → Consequences → Recognition → Outcome
Accidents result from multiple failures in the chain
Early error detection is most effective intervention point
Continuous monitoring and questioning can break the chain

syllabus_desc: Explain clues or red flags that can assist in identifying the error/poor judgement chain.
concept: Red flags indicating entry into the error/poor judgement chain include: feelings of time pressure or urgency, outside pressure to complete a flight, departing from standard procedures without clear justification, repeated small deviations that compound, reluctance to seek help or admit uncertainty, rationalization of risk, dismissal of contradictory information, and tunnel vision or fixation. Physical symptoms like increased heart rate, tension, or fatigue can also indicate approaching judgment lapses. Recognizing these red flags early allows pilots to pause decision-making and apply deliberate judgment processes.
exam_tip: Recognize that red flags are often subtle psychological and situational cues. Look for scenarios showing pressure, rationalization, deviation from norms, or resistance to information.
key_facts: Red flags: time pressure, outside pressure, procedure deviation, compounding errors
Reluctance to seek help or admit uncertainty indicates judgment problems
Rationalization and dismissal of information are warning signs
Physical symptoms can accompany judgment lapses

syllabus_desc: Outline the general concepts behind decision making.
concept: Effective decision-making involves recognizing the need for a decision, identifying available options, evaluating options against criteria (safety, regulatory compliance, feasibility), selecting the best option, implementing it, and monitoring outcomes for feedback. Good decisions rely on accurate information, clear understanding of objectives, awareness of constraints and risks, and explicit consideration of alternatives. Poor decisions often result from inadequate information gathering, failure to consider alternatives, inappropriate time pressure, or premature commitment to a course of action. Pilots benefit from structured decision processes rather than intuitive reactions.
exam_tip: Decision-making is a process, not an event. Understand that good decisions require information gathering, option development, and evaluation—not quick gut reactions.
key_facts: Decision-making is a structured process: recognize → identify → evaluate → choose → implement → monitor
Good decisions require adequate information and consideration of alternatives
Poor decisions often result from incomplete information or unexamined assumptions
Structured processes reduce errors compared to intuitive judgment

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of enhancing decision making skills.
concept: Decision-making skills improve through: formal training in decision models and error recognition, simulation practice with various scenarios, systematic post-flight debriefing and self-analysis, crew resource management emphasizing communication and cross-checking, developing a questioning attitude toward assumptions, establishing personal minimums and decision points, maintaining awareness of personal limitations and fatigue, and seeking feedback from colleagues and instructors. Regular review of accident/incident case studies provides vicarious learning. Building a safety culture emphasizing transparency about errors also enhances collective decision-making.
exam_tip: Methods include training, simulation, debriefing, CRM, questioning assumptions, personal minimums, and feedback seeking. Understand that decision-making improves through deliberate practice and reflection.
key_facts: Training in decision models and error patterns enhances skills
Simulation practice with varied scenarios builds judgment
Systematic debriefing and self-analysis accelerate learning
Personal minimums and decision points provide decision frameworks

syllabus_desc: Explain DECIDE model of decision-making used in aviation.
concept: DECIDE is a six-step decision model: Detect the problem (recognize something is wrong), Estimate the significance (assess how serious it is), Choose a course of action (select best option), Identify the best action (confirm selection against criteria), Do the chosen action (implement decision), Evaluate the results (monitor and adjust). DECIDE provides a systematic approach that reduces errors by ensuring all critical steps are addressed. The model emphasizes detection and estimation as crucial early stages before jumping to action, which helps prevent hasty decisions.
exam_tip: DECIDE means: Detect-Estimate-Choose-Identify-Do-Evaluate. Remember it emphasizes the importance of detecting and estimating the problem before acting.
key_facts: DECIDE: Detect-Estimate-Choose-Identify-Do-Evaluate
Emphasizes early problem detection and significance assessment
Ensures systematic evaluation before action
Includes post-action evaluation and adjustment

syllabus_desc: Explain SADIE model of decision-making used in aviation.
concept: SADIE is a five-step decision model: Scan the situation (gather information about the problem), Analyse the information (interpret and comprehend), Develop options (generate alternatives), Implement the selected option (take action), Evaluate the results (assess outcome and learn). SADIE emphasizes thorough initial information gathering and analysis before committing to action. The model highlights that poor decisions often result from inadequate situation assessment. SADIE is particularly useful for non-emergency decisions where time permits careful analysis.
exam_tip: SADIE means: Scan-Analyse-Develop-Implement-Evaluate. It emphasizes thorough information gathering and analysis as precursors to good decision-making.
key_facts: SADIE: Scan-Analyse-Develop-Implement-Evaluate
Emphasizes comprehensive information gathering and analysis
Develops multiple options before implementation
Useful for non-emergency decisions with adequate time

syllabus_desc: Explain FDODAR model of decision-making used in aviation.
concept: FDODAR is a six-step decision model: Facts (gather accurate information about the situation), Diagnosis (determine the actual problem), Options (generate alternative solutions), Decision (select the best option), Action (implement the decision), Review (evaluate results and adapt). FDODAR emphasizes distinguishing between facts and assumptions, accurate problem identification, and systematic option development. The model's strength is in ensuring correct problem diagnosis before selecting solutions—many errors result from treating symptoms rather than root causes. FDODAR is effective for complex problems requiring careful analysis.
exam_tip: FDODAR means: Facts-Diagnosis-Options-Decision-Action-Review. Key strength is correct problem diagnosis before solution selection.
key_facts: FDODAR: Facts-Diagnosis-Options-Decision-Action-Review
Emphasizes accurate problem diagnosis before solution selection
Distinguishes facts from assumptions
Systematic option development and evaluation

syllabus_desc: Identify specific factors that influence the decision making process.
concept: Multiple factors influence pilot decision-making: time pressure (increases errors through hasty choices), stress and fatigue (degrade cognitive function), workload (reduces available mental resources), experience (can enhance or impair through overconfidence), attitude and mindset (hazardous attitudes bias judgment), information quality and availability (incomplete information leads to errors), organizational and peer pressure (external pressures bias choices), and personal factors like hunger or discomfort. Environmental factors including weather, terrain, and time of day also influence decisions. Understanding these influences allows pilots to recognize when decisions are likely biased and apply more structured approaches.
exam_tip: Recognize how time pressure, stress, fatigue, workload, and external pressure increase decision errors. Personal limitations and attitude also significantly influence choices.
key_facts: Time pressure increases reliance on heuristics and errors
Stress and fatigue degrade decision-making ability
High workload reduces mental resources for analysis
Experience can enhance or impair decisions through overconfidence

syllabus_desc: Explain the setting of personal limitations and decision points.
concept: Personal limitations are restrictions pilots establish based on their training, experience, and ability (weather minimums beyond regulatory requirements, maximum crosswind limits, terrain avoidance margins). Decision points are predetermined conditions or aircraft states at which pilots commit to specific actions (e.g., 'if weather deteriorates below VFR minima, divert to alternate'). Setting personal limitations and decision points beforehand prevents in-flight decisions from being influenced by get-home-itis or other pressure. These predetermined choices provide objective decision criteria that bypass emotion-laden judgment. Effective pilots consistently identify their personal limitations for each flight and establish clear decision points.
exam_tip: Personal limitations are individual restrictions exceeding regulatory minimums based on pilot capability. Decision points are predetermined conditions triggering specific actions. Both prevent pressure-influenced decisions.
key_facts: Personal limitations are individual restrictions based on training and ability
Personal minimums typically exceed regulatory requirements
Decision points are predetermined conditions triggering specific actions
Predetermined decisions resist pressure and emotion-laden judgment

syllabus_desc: Outline the dangers of get-home-itis.
concept: Get-home-itis is the hazardous mental state of being determined to complete a flight despite deteriorating conditions or safety concerns. It results from external pressure (schedule, passenger expectations, business needs) or internal pressure (ego, proving capability). Get-home-itis causes pilots to make poor risk assessment decisions, depart with inadequate weather margins, continue into deteriorating conditions, or attempt recovery from unusual attitudes. It represents loss of objectivity about risk and is a significant factor in weather-related and loss-of-control accidents. Prevention requires recognizing the condition in oneself, establishing firm personal minimums before flight, and maintaining willingness to divert or delay.
exam_tip: Get-home-itis is pressure-induced poor judgment. Key prevention: establish personal minimums before flight and follow them objectively regardless of pressure.
key_facts: Get-home-itis is determination to complete a flight despite deteriorating conditions
Results from external and internal pressure
Causes poor risk assessment and continued flight into hazards
Prevents effective diversion and alternate planning decisions

topic: 10.50 — Social Psychology and Flight Deck Management
syllabus_desc: Explain how outside resources, such as ATC, engineers and other pilots can contribute to a pilot’s management of a flight.
concept: External resources enhance pilot decision-making and safety management. Air Traffic Control provides weather information, traffic advisories, and alternative routing. Flight engineers or flight attendants provide additional information and cross-checking capability. Other pilots (through crew resource management) offer diverse perspectives, catch errors, and provide moral support. Maintenance personnel provide accurate aircraft status. Ground support services (meteorology, dispatch) provide strategic information for flight planning. Effective pilots actively utilize these resources by clearly communicating needs, asking for assistance without hesitation, and integrating outside input into decisions. Barriers to resource utilization include over-confidence, poor communication, or failure to recognize personal limitations.
exam_tip: Resource utilization is central to modern flight management. Understand that seeking help and input from ATC, crew, and support is not weakness but sound judgment.
key_facts: ATC provides weather, traffic, and routing information
Crew members provide additional information and error checking
Dispatch and meteorology provide strategic flight planning support
Effective resource utilization requires clear communication and willingness to accept input

syllabus_desc: Explain the role of human error in aviation accidents.
concept: Human error is implicated in approximately 80% of aviation accidents. Errors occur in all types of aviation operations and across all experience levels. Human error typically involves skill-based errors (slips and lapses during routine procedures), decision errors (poor judgment or risk assessment), or perceptual errors (misinterpretation of information). Most errors do not directly cause accidents; instead, accidents occur when errors combine with system failures or when error detection and recovery mechanisms fail. Understanding error as a normal and inevitable aspect of human operation, rather than a moral failing, enables development of systems that detect and mitigate errors.
exam_tip: Human error causes ~80% of accidents, but errors are normal. Focus on error detection and recovery systems rather than error elimination as unrealistic.
key_facts: Human error is implicated in approximately 80% of accidents
Error is inevitable given human cognitive limitations
Skill-based, decision, and perceptual errors all contribute
Accidents result from error plus system failure, not error alone

syllabus_desc: Explain the degree to which human error can be eliminated.
concept: Human error cannot be completely eliminated because it is inherent to human cognition and performance. The limitations of attention, memory, and perception ensure that errors will occur. However, the frequency, magnitude, and consequence of errors can be substantially reduced through: better training and procedure design, appropriate workload management, improved information display and decision support, peer checking and crew resource management, fatigue management, and personal skill development. The focus in modern aviation is not on eliminating human error (impossible goal) but on designing systems that recognize error as inevitable and provide multiple layers of error detection, containment, and recovery. This approach has proven far more effective than expecting errorless performance.
exam_tip: Error cannot be eliminated—it's inherent to human cognition. Instead, design systems that detect and recover from errors. This is the Swiss Cheese Model principle.
key_facts: Human error cannot be completely eliminated
Error results from inherent cognitive limitations
Focus is on error detection and recovery, not elimination
Systems designed with multiple defensive layers are most effective

syllabus_desc: Describe the main types of threats which could potentially affect a safe flight.
concept: Threats in aviation can be categorized as environmental (weather, terrain, traffic), organisational (scheduling pressures, inadequate training, maintenance issues), and external (fuel availability, airport closures). The Threat and Error Management (TEM) framework treats threats as external factors that increase error likelihood and accident risk. Environmental threats include thunderstorms, crosswinds, or mountainous terrain. Organisational threats include inadequate crew rest, high workload design, or pressure to complete flights. Pilots cannot eliminate threats but can manage them through recognition, avoidance, and mitigation. TEM uses a chain model: Threats increase error likelihood, errors create undesired aircraft states, and undesired states can lead to accidents if not recovered.
exam_tip: Know the TEM chain: Threats → increase error likelihood → Errors → create undesired states → potential accidents. Threat management is primary prevention.
key_facts: Environmental threats: weather, terrain, traffic
Organisational threats: scheduling, training, maintenance
External threats: fuel, airport availability
Threat management through recognition, avoidance, and mitigation

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic elements and features of the Reason Model.
concept: The Reason Model (Swiss Cheese Model) depicts aviation safety as multiple layers of defense, each represented as a slice of Swiss cheese with holes. Accidents occur only when holes in multiple layers align, allowing an error to propagate through to cause an accident. The model includes layers such as pilot training, procedures, aircraft design, air traffic control, and maintenance. Each layer is meant to catch and prevent errors. No single layer is perfect (holes exist), but when properly designed, multiple layers make accident propagation unlikely. The model recognizes that accidents are system failures, not just individual errors. It explains why most errors do not cause accidents (defensive layers catch them) but emphasizes that a few errors bypass all defenses.
exam_tip: Swiss Cheese Model shows multiple defensive layers. Understand that accidents require alignment of holes in multiple layers, not just a single error.
key_facts: Multiple defensive layers like training, procedures, design, ATC, maintenance
Accidents occur when holes in multiple layers align
No single layer is perfect, but layering reduces accident risk
System design is critical; accidents are failures across multiple layers

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management, including means of recognising threats.
concept: Threat recognition requires systematic observation and analysis of the flight environment, aircraft state, and operational factors. Key recognition strategies include: regular instrument scanning (vertical speed, altitude, airspeed, attitude), cross-checking instruments for discrepancies, monitoring weather and conditions, tracking fuel and time remaining, evaluating crew capability and fatigue, and questioning deviations from planned profile. Recognizing a threat depends on attentiveness and mental models that flag anomalies. Threats are often subtle (gradual wind shift, slow altitude change) and can be masked by attention to other tasks. Crew communication and cross-checking improve threat recognition by providing diverse perspectives. Personal threats (fatigue, stress) and aircraft system threats (equipment failures) also require recognition.
exam_tip: Recognition requires active monitoring, systematic scanning, cross-checking, and questioning anomalies. Passive observation misses subtle threats.
key_facts: Recognition requires systematic observation of aircraft, environment, and operations
Regular instrument scanning and cross-checking identify discrepancies
Threats can be subtle and masked by task focus
Crew communication and diverse perspectives improve recognition

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management, including means of avoiding threats.
concept: Threat avoidance involves planning to prevent exposure to threats and making real-time decisions to stay away from identified threats. Pre-flight threat avoidance includes: reviewing weather and planning routes around storms, checking aircraft maintenance status, briefing alternate airports before weather deteriorates, and ensuring crew is adequately rested. In-flight avoidance includes: requesting weather deviations from ATC to avoid thunderstorms, climbing over icing layers rather than through them, maintaining safe separation from terrain and traffic, and diverting when approaching personal minimums. Avoidance is more effective than mitigation because it prevents escalation of the threat. Effective avoidance requires recognizing threats early and making decisions while options remain.
exam_tip: Avoidance is primary defense. Pre-flight planning (weather review, alternates) prevents many threats. In-flight decisions must be made early to preserve options.
key_facts: Pre-flight avoidance through weather planning and alternate selection
In-flight avoidance: weather deviations, altitude changes, diversion
Avoidance is more effective than recovery
Early threat recognition preserves avoidance options

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management, including means of mitigating the effects of threats.
concept: Threat mitigation involves reducing the severity or consequence of a threat that cannot be avoided. Mitigation strategies include: increasing safety margins (descending to ensure terrain clearance even when weather limits visibility), reducing speed in challenging conditions, increasing crew workload distribution, using automation to reduce human workload during high threat periods, cross-checking systems for failures, and maintaining aircraft configuration for maximum performance. Mitigation may involve accepting reduced efficiency (longer flight times, lower altitudes) in exchange for increased safety. Examples include reducing speed in turbulence or icing, extending landing distance requirements for crosswind operations, or declaring an emergency to access priority handling. Mitigation is less ideal than avoidance but more realistic when threats are unavoidable.
exam_tip: Mitigation means reducing threat consequence when avoidance isn't possible. Examples: reduce speed, increase margins, manage workload, use checklists.
key_facts: Mitigation increases safety margins when avoidance isn't possible
Strategies include speed reduction, margin increase, and workload distribution
Automation and checklists reduce error during high-threat periods
Mitigation often requires accepting reduced efficiency for safety

syllabus_desc: Identify the elements in a safety culture.
concept: A strong safety culture comprises four elements: Just Culture (fair treatment of error without blame for good-faith actions), Reporting Culture (willingness to report errors and hazards without fear of punishment), Learning Culture (systematic analysis of incidents and errors to improve systems), and Informed Culture (knowledge of safety risks and mitigation strategies throughout the organization). Just Culture distinguishes between normal error (inevitable, not punishable), at-risk behavior (choice to deviate from standards, addressable through retraining), and high culpability behavior (reckless or willfully negligent, requires disciplinary action). These elements work together: Reporting Culture creates data for Learning Culture, Just Culture encourages honest reporting, and Informed Culture prevents hazards.
exam_tip: Four elements: Just, Reporting, Learning, Informed. Understand how they interact: Just encourages Reporting, which feeds Learning, which builds Informed culture.
key_facts: Just Culture: fair treatment distinguishing error from misconduct
Reporting Culture: willingness to report without fear
Learning Culture: systematic analysis and improvement
Informed Culture: knowledge of risks and mitigations

syllabus_desc: List the key reasons for safety reporting in aviation.
concept: Safety reporting serves critical functions: incident data identifies hazards and unsafe patterns before accidents occur, allowing proactive mitigation; data from reports reveals system weaknesses rather than just individual errors; trending of incidents shows emerging safety risks; near-miss reporting captures 'free lessons' from events that resulted in no harm; feedback to the pilot or crew creates a learning loop that changes future behavior; and organizational learning from aggregated reports prevents recurrence of similar incidents. Aviation regulatory authorities analyze safety reports to identify industry-wide patterns and issues systemic interventions. Without structured safety reporting, organizations operate without essential safety data and repeat preventable errors.
exam_tip: Reporting captures free lessons before accidents occur. Data identifies system weaknesses, enables proactive intervention, and drives organizational learning.
key_facts: Reports identify hazards and unsafe patterns before accidents
Reveals system weaknesses and latent error conditions
Trending shows emerging risks requiring intervention
Creates organizational learning loop preventing recurrence

syllabus_desc: Explain the rationale for mandatory reporting of incidents as required by Part 12.
concept: New Zealand Civil Aviation Part 12 requires mandatory reporting of aviation occurrences (accidents, serious incidents, and incidents) to establish a comprehensive database for system analysis and improvement. Mandatory reporting ensures that significant events are not hidden or unreported due to liability concerns, organizational preference, or individual reluctance. The mandatory system captures safety data across all operators and creates consistent information for regulatory analysis. Legal protections in just-culture frameworks (non-punitive treatment of honest reports) support reporting. Mandatory reporting rationale includes: establishing factual baseline of aviation risks, identifying emerging hazards, enabling comparative analysis across operators, and driving systemic improvements. The data supports evidence-based safety policy and resource allocation.
exam_tip: Mandatory reporting (Part 12) ensures comprehensive safety data collection and prevents organizations from hiding incidents. It supports evidence-based safety improvements.
key_facts: Part 12 requires reporting of accidents, serious incidents, and incidents
Mandatory system creates comprehensive safety database
Prevents individual incidents from being hidden
Data supports evidence-based safety policy and improvements

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between normal error, at risk behaviour and high culpability behaviour.
concept: Just Culture distinguishes three categories: Normal Error includes slips (execution failures in planned actions) and lapses (memory failures to execute planned actions). These are inevitable given human cognitive limits and not subject to disciplinary action, only system improvement and retraining. At-Risk Behavior involves a deliberate choice to deviate from standards but without intention to harm (e.g., skipping a checklist to save time, violating a procedure because it seems unnecessary). At-risk behavior is preventable and requires retraining, increased awareness, or procedural redesign. High Culpability Behavior involves reckless or willfully negligent actions (e.g., flying while intoxicated, deliberate aircraft misuse, or violation of clear rules). High culpability behavior requires investigation, disciplinary action, and possible legal consequences. The distinctions prevent unfair punishment of inevitable errors while maintaining accountability for preventable violations.
exam_tip: Normal error = inevitable, not punished. At-risk = choice to deviate, needs retraining. High culpability = reckless/willful, requires discipline.
key_facts: Normal Error: inevitable slips and lapses; system improvement only
At-Risk Behavior: choice to deviate from standards; preventable through retraining
High Culpability: reckless or willful violations; requires investigation and discipline
Distinctions enable fair accountability and learning

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between negligent and reckless behaviour.
concept: Negligent behavior involves failure to exercise reasonable care or due diligence, resulting in harmful consequences. Negligence typically involves an unintended outcome from careless or inattentive action (e.g., failing to maintain altitude due to distraction, not conducting proper preflight inspection). Reckless behavior involves deliberate disregard for danger or clear knowledge of risk combined with willful action despite that knowledge (e.g., flying below minimums despite deteriorating weather, continuing with known equipment malfunction). Both are forms of high culpability behavior, but recklessness implies greater intentionality and awareness of danger. Legal and regulatory consequences differ: negligence may result from insufficient training or poor procedures (system issues), while recklessness indicates decision to accept known risks and typically demands individual accountability.
exam_tip: Negligent = careless unintended harm. Reckless = deliberate disregard of known dangers. Both serious, but recklessness is more willful.
key_facts: Negligent: failure to exercise reasonable care; unintended harmful outcome
Reckless: deliberate disregard for danger; knowing risk acceptance
Both are high culpability behaviors requiring investigation
Recklessness implies greater intentionality and awareness

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of punitive sanction.
concept: Punitive sanctions (disciplinary action, fines, license suspension) serve several purposes in aviation safety: they provide deterrence against deliberate violations and high-culpability behavior, they establish clear organizational expectations about unacceptable behavior, and they demonstrate accountability to the public and regulatory authorities. However, in a just culture, sanctions are applied narrowly to high-culpability behavior only. Applying sanctions to inevitable errors or at-risk behavior actually reduces safety by discouraging reporting and learning. Over-reliance on punishment creates a punitive culture that drives incidents underground rather than into safety reporting systems. Effective use of sanctions requires: clear distinction between culpability levels, transparent application consistent with policy, proportionality between violation and sanction, and primary focus on system improvement rather than blame. Sanctions are a tool for accountability, not a safety strategy.
exam_tip: Sanctions are appropriate for high-culpability behavior but must be applied fairly and rarely. Over-punishment drives incidents underground and reduces reporting.
key_facts: Sanctions serve deterrence and accountability for high-culpability behavior
Sanctions on normal errors discourage reporting and worsen safety
Proportional, consistent application maintains culture credibility
System improvement is primary focus; sanctions are secondary

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts:
concept: Display instruments present flight information in various formats to support pilot perception and decision-making. Analog displays use needle position relative to scale markings to show values (altimeter, airspeed). Digital displays show numerical values directly (altitude readouts). Integrated displays present multiple data on a single screen with visual relationships (Primary Flight Display showing attitude, airspeed, altitude, heading in one glance). Trend displays show rate of change (vertical speed indicator). Context matters: analog displays provide good trend and rate information through needle movement but can be misread; digital displays are precise but provide poor trend information; integrated displays reduce pilot scanning but require complex interpretation. Modern aircraft use combinations: analog instruments for backup, digital for precise values, and integrated displays for comprehensive situational awareness. Effective display design reduces pilot workload and error through appropriate information presentation.
exam_tip: Different display types suit different purposes. Analog shows trends well, digital shows exact values, integrated provides comprehensive SA. Understand the tradeoffs.
key_facts: Analog displays: needle position shows value and trend
Digital displays: exact numerical values but poor trend visibility
Integrated displays: multiple data presented with visual relationships
Display design affects workload, scanning, and interpretation accuracy

syllabus_desc: Describe parallax error.
concept: Alerting systems (warnings, cautions, advisories) have inherent limitations: alerts cannot distinguish between actual hazards and false alarms reliably (high false alarm rates reduce credibility and lead to desensitization), alerts may be triggered too late to permit recovery (late warnings are useless), alert thresholds may be set too conservative (excessive nuisance alerts) or too lenient (missed real hazards), alerts do not automatically capture attention if pilots are task-focused (change blindness), and accumulation of simultaneous alerts creates confusion and workload. Pilots may experience alert fatigue if they encounter frequent false alarms, causing them to ignore or minimize alert significance. Additionally, some hazards (loss of airspeed, spatial disorientation) may develop so rapidly that no alert system provides sufficient warning. Effective alerting requires careful threshold setting, pilot training in interpretation, and integration with other information sources. Alerts should guide attention but not replace active monitoring.
exam_tip: Alerting limitations: false alarms, late warnings, high workload from multiple alerts, and alert fatigue. Understand that alerts support but cannot replace pilot monitoring.
key_facts: False alarms reduce alert credibility and trigger desensitization
Late warnings provide insufficient time for recovery
Multiple simultaneous alerts create confusion and increased workload
Some hazards develop too rapidly for effective alerting

syllabus_desc: Describe potential errors in the interpretation of three pointer altimeters.
concept: Common instrument reading errors include: transposition errors (reading 3000 feet as 3000 feet when actually at 5000 feet), decimal errors (reading 150 knots as 1500 knots), reversal errors (reading an inverted indicator backwards), scale misidentification (reading the wrong scale on multi-scale instruments), parallax errors (reading from incorrect viewing angle), fixation errors (reading only one instrument and missing discrepancies), and interpolation errors (guessing values between scale marks incorrectly). Expectancy effects cause pilots to 'see' expected values rather than actual values. Fatigue and workload increase error rates. Instruments vary in error susceptibility: analog instruments are prone to parallax and misidentification, digital displays to transposition of similar-looking digits, and integrated displays to misinterpretation of relationships. Cross-checking instruments and explicit reading protocols (calling out values) reduce errors.
exam_tip: Common errors: transposition, decimal, reversal, scale mix-up, parallax, fixation, interpolation. Workload and expectancy worsen errors. Cross-checking is primary defense.
key_facts: Transposition errors: reading wrong position or order of digits
Decimal errors: misplacing decimal point
Expectancy errors: seeing expected value instead of actual
Workload and fatigue increase all instrument reading errors

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic requirements of alerts.
concept: Instrument interpretation errors are minimized through several techniques: standardized scanning patterns ensure all critical instruments are checked systematically; explicit verbal or written callouts of instrument values create a secondary check and encode values in memory better than silent reading; cross-checking related instruments (airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, attitude) for consistency catches errors where one instrument is misread; redundant instruments provide backup if one is misread; digital readouts for critical values reduce interpretation errors compared to analog readings; training in common error types increases awareness and selective attention to error-prone instruments; and adequate workload management ensures sufficient attention for accurate reading. Procedures that require verification of critical values (confirming altitude assignments, verifying heading changes) create additional error detection points. In high-workload situations, explicit challenge-response confirmation procedures (pilot says altitude, observer confirms) are especially effective.
exam_tip: Minimize errors through: systematic scanning, cross-checking, callouts, redundancy, training on common errors, and adequate workload management.
key_facts: Systematic scanning ensures all instruments checked regularly
Explicit callouts create secondary check and memory encoding
Cross-checking related instruments catches discrepancies
Redundant instruments and digital displays reduce misreading

syllabus_desc: Describe how colour coding conventions are used in aviation on instruments and displays.
concept: Ergonomic cockpit design applies human factors principles to cockpit layout and instrument arrangement to minimize error and workload. Key ergonomic concepts include: spatial organization (most frequently used controls and displays positioned for easy access), standardization (consistent control location and operation across aircraft types), logical grouping (related displays and controls positioned together), appropriate sizing and contrast (displays readable from normal viewing distance), glare reduction (display brightness controlled relative to ambient light), and minimizing cognitive load (information presented clearly with appropriate meaning rather than raw data). Ergonomic design considers pilot reach and vision, reduces scanning distance between related instruments, and arranges controls by frequency of use. Modern glass cockpits apply ergonomic principles through customizable displays allowing pilots to arrange information according to task phase. Poor ergonomic design increases scanning workload, raises error rates, and contributes to accidents. Regulatory standards and best practices guide cockpit design.
exam_tip: Ergonomic design reduces workload and error through proper spatial organization, standardization, appropriate display sizing, and logical information grouping.
key_facts: Spatial organization: frequent-use items easily accessible
Standardization: consistent control location across aircraft types
Logical grouping: related displays and controls together
Information design: clear presentation minimizing cognitive load

syllabus_desc: Explain the rationale behind consistent and thorough checklist use as opposed to reliance on memory.
concept: Checklists serve multiple essential purposes in aviation: they externalize memory requirements, preventing omission of critical items through systematic verification; they standardize procedures across pilots and aircraft, ensuring consistency and reducing variability; they serve as cross-checking tools, allowing one pilot to verify another's actions; they document approved procedures, maintaining continuity during crew changes or across operations; and they adapt procedures to specific conditions (normal, abnormal, emergency). Checklists compensate for working memory limitations, fatigue, distraction, and high workload. Studies demonstrate that proper checklist use reduces error rates substantially compared to reliance on memory. Effective checklists are brief, focused on critical items, and structured for challenge-response verification. Checklists are not memory crutches but safety systems that recognize human limitations and design around them.
exam_tip: Checklists overcome memory limitations through external verification. Proper use means challenge-response completion with item-by-item verification.
key_facts: Externalize memory requirements preventing omission errors
Standardize procedures across pilots and aircraft
Enable cross-checking and mutual verification
Compensate for workload, fatigue, and distraction

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of normal checklists.
concept: Normal checklists provide systematic procedures for standard flight operations: pre-flight inspection, pre-start, engine start, before-takeoff, after-takeoff, cruise, descent, approach, and landing. Normal checklists verify that systems are configured correctly, required checks have been completed, and aircraft is ready for each flight phase. They follow a standard format: challenge (pilot reads item), response (second pilot or first pilot verifies), and confirmation (action is taken). Normal checklists are used every flight and should be brief, focusing on essential checks rather than exhaustive system review. Well-designed normal checklists take 2-5 minutes per flight phase. Pilots must understand the purpose of each checklist item to use it effectively and recognize when something is wrong. Normal checklist discipline establishes habit patterns that carry through to abnormal situations.
exam_tip: Normal checklists are routine, systematic verification of aircraft configuration. Challenge-response format is key. Must be used every flight consistently.
key_facts: Systematic verification for each flight phase
Challenge-response format ensures verification
Brief, focused on essential items only
Habit formation supports safety in abnormal situations

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of emergency checklists.
concept: Emergency checklists provide procedures for handling system failures, abnormal situations, and true emergencies. They are used when specific malfunctions or emergencies are declared. Emergency checklists prioritize safety and basic aircraft control, providing step-by-step procedures to diagnose the problem and manage it. They often include memory items (critical steps to accomplish immediately from memory before reading checklist) and subsequent detailed steps. Emergency checklists must be studied during regular training so pilots can execute memory items without consulting the checklist. Effective emergency checklists prevent fixation (continued attempts at impossible solutions) and ensure pilots consider all recovery options. Emergency checklists should be validated through training and simulation before actual use. The organization and clarity of emergency checklists are especially critical because pilots may be executing them during high stress and cognitive load.
exam_tip: Emergency checklists include memory items (immediate actions) plus detailed steps. Must be trained until memory items are automatic.
key_facts: Step-by-step procedures for abnormal and emergency situations
Memory items provide critical initial actions
Detailed steps follow after immediate actions
Training and simulation validation essential before operational use

syllabus_desc: Identify the phases of flight where a checklist plays an important role.
concept: Checklists, while essential, are insufficient in several situations: checklist-driven operations can lead to over-reliance on procedure compliance without understanding underlying principles, leaving pilots unable to improvise when situations don't fit standard procedures. Unplanned emergencies or combinations of failures may not have matching checklists, requiring problem-solving beyond procedure following. Checklist fixation can occur when pilots focus entirely on completing a checklist item while ignoring a more critical developing emergency (e.g., focusing on a landing checklist while losing engine power). Checklists assume pilots understand what items mean and why they're important; inadequate training can result in checklist completion without actual verification. Novel situations, combination failures, or degraded system states may require judgment and understanding beyond procedure. Additionally, checklists must be maintained and updated; outdated checklists can lead pilots astray. Effective operations require integrating checklist discipline with understanding, flexible thinking, and situational awareness.
exam_tip: Checklists + judgment and understanding = safety. Checklist-only approach fails with novel situations, unplanned combinations, or fixation.
key_facts: Checklist fixation can cause missing critical developments
Unplanned emergencies may lack matching checklists
Over-reliance on procedures without understanding is dangerous
Understanding of underlying principles enables adaptation

syllabus_desc: Describe the reasons for checklist complacency.
concept: Common checklist errors include: omission errors (skipping items due to rushing or distraction), verification errors (checking off items without actually verifying they're complete), sequence errors (completing items in wrong order), continuation errors (stopping checklist prematurely), and selection errors (using wrong checklist for the situation). Memory errors can occur when pilots remember checklist items incorrectly or confuse similar items from different checklists. Checklist misuse includes challenge-response failures (reader and responder not clear on roles), distraction during checklist (interruptions causing loss of place), and checklist manipulation (deliberately circumventing checks to save time). Environmental factors (noise, poor lighting, turbulence) can increase checklist errors. High workload and time pressure increase all error types. Training in proper checklist discipline, explicit procedures for challenge-response, and cultural emphasis on never skipping checklist items are primary defenses.
exam_tip: Errors: omission, verification failure, sequence errors, distraction, improper role definition. Proper discipline and training prevent most errors.
key_facts: Omission: skipping items due to rushing or distraction
Verification failure: checking off without actual confirmation
Challenge-response breakdown: reader/responder role confusion
Distraction and interruption cause loss of place in checklist

syllabus_desc: Describe the possible ramifications of checklist complacency.
concept: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) define how operations will be conducted, providing standardization across the organization, reducing variability, and establishing best practices. SOPs ensure that pilots follow proven, safe methods rather than developing individual techniques. They facilitate training by providing explicit standards pilots must learn. SOPs allow coordination: when all pilots follow the same procedures, crew resource management and multi-pilot operations function more smoothly. SOPs also support regulatory compliance, demonstrating to authorities that the organization operates according to approved standards. SOPs are typically more comprehensive than checklists, covering sequences of actions, decision points, and reasoning. Effective SOPs are based on operational experience, validated through testing and simulation, and regularly updated as equipment or understanding changes. SOPs balance prescription (ensuring safety-critical actions are performed consistently) with flexibility (allowing reasonable adaptation to different circumstances).
exam_tip: SOPs standardize operations across pilots, facilitate training, enable crew coordination, and ensure regulatory compliance. They provide detailed procedures beyond checklist items.
key_facts: Standardize procedures across pilots and organization
Facilitate training by providing explicit standards
Enable crew coordination and multi-pilot operations
Ensure regulatory compliance and safety consistency

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for shock.
concept: Shock is a life-threatening condition where the body's systems fail to maintain adequate circulation and oxygen delivery. First aid treatment focuses on maintaining circulation and preventing further deterioration: lay the casualty flat or slightly reclined to maintain cerebral blood flow; elevate lower extremities slightly (about 30 cm) to redirect blood toward vital organs; loosen tight clothing restricting circulation; maintain body temperature by removing wet clothing and covering with blankets; keep casualty calm through reassurance and explanation; avoid movement and physical exertion; if casualty is conscious, position on side to maintain airway if unconscious; provide oxygen if available; and arrange immediate evacuation for medical care. Critical actions include recognizing shock early (restlessness, rapid pulse, pale cold skin, weakness) and treating it before cardiovascular collapse occurs. Shock accompanying major bleeding requires rapid bleeding control as primary treatment.
exam_tip: Shock treatment: lay flat or reclined, elevate legs, warm, reassure, and evacuate rapidly. Early recognition and treatment are essential.
key_facts: Lay flat or slightly reclined to maintain cerebral circulation
Elevate lower extremities to redirect blood to vital organs
Maintain body temperature through appropriate clothing
Rapid evacuation and medical treatment are essential

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for burns.
concept: Burns are injuries from heat, flames, chemicals, or electrical current. First aid treatment depends on burn severity and extent: immediately stop the burning process by removing clothing (unless adherent to skin) and using cool running water to reduce heat and prevent further tissue damage; do not apply ice directly (risks frostbite); cover with clean dry dressing to prevent infection; do not apply creams or ointments; for large or severe burns, cool with large volumes of water, remove constrictive items (jewelry, belts), elevate affected limbs, and arrange immediate evacuation. Pain management is important. Chemical burns require removal of contaminating clothing and prolonged rinsing. Electrical burns may have entry and exit wounds plus internal damage; treat for shock and arrange medical evaluation. Burns covering more than 15% body surface (rule of 9s) are major medical emergencies requiring immediate evacuation and specialized treatment.
exam_tip: Burn treatment: cool with water immediately, cover with clean dressing, avoid creams/ointments, assess severity (>15% requires emergency evacuation).
key_facts: Cool immediately with running water to stop heat damage
Cover with clean dry dressing to prevent infection
Do not apply ice, creams, or ointments
Large burns (>15% body surface) require immediate evacuation

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for cuts and bleeding.
concept: Cuts and bleeding are common injuries with treatment varying by severity. For minor cuts and bleeding: wash the wound with clean water, apply pressure with clean cloth to control bleeding, elevate the injured part if possible, and apply antibiotic ointment and appropriate dressing. For significant bleeding: apply firm direct pressure with cloth, maintain pressure for 10-15 minutes (do not repeatedly check bleeding), elevate the injured part, and apply additional layers of cloth if first soaks through (do not remove first layer, as this disrupts clotting). For severe bleeding: apply direct pressure, elevate, apply pressure to pressure points if necessary, use tourniquet as last resort only for life-threatening limb hemorrhage, and arrange immediate evacuation. All but the most minor wounds should be evaluated medically. Infection risk increases with dirty wounds or delayed care; tetanus status should be considered.
exam_tip: Bleeding control: direct pressure (maintain, don't check), elevation, pressure points if needed, tourniquet only for life-threatening limb hemorrhage.
key_facts: Direct pressure is primary bleeding control method
Elevate injured part to assist pressure
Pressure points and tourniquets for severe bleeding
Significant bleeding requires medical evaluation

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic principles of Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation.
concept: First aid kits on board aircraft serve critical functions in remote area operations where medical care may be hours or days away. A properly equipped first aid kit enables treatment of common injuries and medical emergencies, potentially preventing minor injuries from becoming serious or life-threatening. Kits typically include: bandages and dressings for cuts and wounds, elastic bandages for sprains, antibiotic ointment, pain relievers, medications for common ailments (antacids, antihistamines), sterile gauze and tape, tourniquets, trauma shears, burn treatment supplies, and basic trauma supplies. For remote operations, additional supplies appropriate to the environment (cold weather, tropical, desert) should be considered. The kit is only useful if: contents are regularly checked for expiration and replacement, the pilot knows what's in the kit and how to use items, and the kit is accessible during emergency situations. In survival situations, first aid kit contents may be essential for managing injuries sustained during crash landing or in harsh environments.
exam_tip: First aid kits are essential for remote operations. Understand basic contents and why they're important: enabling treatment when professional medical care is unavailable.
key_facts: Enables treatment of injuries when medical care is distant
Prevents minor injuries from becoming serious
Must be regularly maintained and contents verified
Pilot must know contents and basic usage

syllabus_desc: State the components of a pre-flight passenger briefing by a pilot with respect to aircraft safety features and equipment.
concept: Survival priorities in remote areas follow a specific sequence that maximizes survival probability: Protection (shelter, clothing, fire) is the first priority—exposure kills faster than hunger or dehydration; Location (signaling, attracting rescue) is the second priority—rescue cannot occur if rescuers cannot find survivors; Water is the third priority—dehydration impairs judgment and physical capability; Food is the last priority—humans can survive weeks without food but only days without water. This priority sequence is counterintuitive (many assume finding food is first) but reflects physiological reality. Immediate actions include: securing shelter from elements, signaling rescuers (mirrors, bright colors, fires), obtaining water, and only then seeking food. The priorities are reflected in survival training and represent hard-won knowledge from survival incidents.
exam_tip: Survival priorities: Protection (shelter/clothing), Location (signaling), Water, Food. Remember this sequence—protection and location are more critical than immediate food gathering.
key_facts: Protection (shelter, clothing, warmth) highest priority
Location (signaling for rescue) second priority
Water third priority
Food last priority

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic steps in post-crash survivor management.
concept: Flights over remote areas where emergency landing would result in isolation require specific preparation to maximize survival probability. Preparation includes: current weather and terrain knowledge, detailed flight planning with alternate routes and landing sites, aircraft survival equipment (emergency locator transmitter, survival kit, first aid supplies, water, signaling equipment), personal equipment (appropriate clothing, navigation aids, communication devices), fuel planning ensuring adequate reserves for routing changes or diversion, knowledge of terrain and environmental hazards, and contingency plans for various failure scenarios. Pre-flight brief should address: emergency procedures, crew responsibilities during emergency landing, location of survival equipment, and post-landing priorities (signaling, protection, water). Aircraft maintenance must be rigorous because mechanical failure in remote areas creates true survival situations. Recent communication with authorities establishing expected arrival time and procedures for reporting overdue status is essential. Preparation reflects the principle that survival begins before departure and continues through planning and equipment selection.
exam_tip: Remote area preparation requires: detailed planning, survival equipment, personal gear, fuel reserves, terrain knowledge, and contingency procedures. Survival begins before departure.
key_facts: Detailed flight planning with alternate landing sites
Adequate survival equipment and supplies on board
Personal equipment appropriate to environment
Fuel reserves and contingency procedures for diversion

syllabus_desc: List the priorities of survival in order of importance.
concept: Water procurement in survival situations relies on multiple methods: collection from rainfall through cloths or tarps, finding natural water sources (streams, lakes, seepage areas), extracting moisture from plant sources (cutting vines, squeezing water from broad leaves in tropical areas), and solar stills using transparent plastic and ground moisture in desert areas. Water found in nature should be purified by boiling or chemical treatment to prevent waterborne illness. Food procurement depends on environment and skills: insects and grubs provide protein in most environments, edible plants vary by location (knowledge of local flora is essential), fishing using improvised methods, trapping small animals using snares or deadfalls, and harvesting of eggs or birds if accessible. In survival training emphasis is on realistic food sources available immediately rather than hunting large game. Knowledge of what's edible is essential because many plants are toxic. Caloric intake in survival situations is often inadequate, so rationing available resources and finding sufficient water is more critical than finding abundant food.
exam_tip: Water: rainfall collection, natural sources, plant extraction, solar stills. Food: insects, edible plants (must know which), fishing, trapping. Water is priority.
key_facts: Water collection from rainfall, natural sources, and plants
Water purification through boiling or chemical treatment
Food sources: insects, edible plants, fishing, trapping
Knowledge of local plants is essential to avoid poisoning

syllabus_desc: Explain survival items that could be carried on a cross-country flight over bush clad and mountainous terrain in order to manage survival threats.
concept: Shelter in survival situations must address the primary threat: in cold environments, shelter provides insulation and heat retention; in hot environments, shade and ventilation prevent heat death; in wet environments, protection from rain and ground moisture prevents hypothermia. Basic shelter requirements include: protection from wind and precipitation, insulation from ground (ground contact rapidly drains body heat), maintenance of body temperature (warmth in cold, cooling in heat), and location visibility for rescue. Construction depends on available materials and environment: in forest areas, lean-to structures with branches and leaves; in open areas, use aircraft fuselage for shelter and wind protection; in hot climates, shade structure with air circulation; in water survival, flotation device becomes shelter. Shelter location is critical: visible to rescue aircraft while providing protection, away from hazards (avalanche terrain, flood zones), and near resources (fresh water, firewood). Insulation materials (leaves, grass, bark) should be gathered before darkness falls. In remote survival situations, shelter construction becomes first priority after emergency landing.
exam_tip: Shelter basics: protection from elements, insulation, heat retention/dissipation depending on climate, visibility for rescue. Build before darkness.
key_facts: Insulation from ground and protection from wind/rain
Materials depend on environment (forest, open, tropical)
Location must balance protection with rescue visibility
Construction before darkness maximizes safety

syllabus_desc: Explain the process of hypothermia.
concept: Signaling rescuers is essential because rescue cannot occur if survivors cannot be located. Signaling methods vary by situation and available resources: visual signals (bright colors, mirrors reflecting sunlight, smoke from fires) are effective for aircraft or distant observers; audible signals (three 'SOS' blasts on aircraft horn, whistles) help ground rescuers locate survivors nearby; radio communication (emergency locator transmitter, survival radio) provides most reliable signaling for modern rescue; ground-to-air signals using geometric patterns (SOS, X, signal fire) guide rescuers; mirrors can signal aircraft from impressive distances in clear weather; and bright colors (orange, red, yellow) increase visibility of clothing and shelters. Signaling strategy should use multiple methods concurrently: maintain signaling fire, position bright materials for visibility, use radio if operational, and prepare visual signals for aircraft. Location of survival equipment (mirrors, bright materials, signaling devices) must be known before survival situation occurs. The key principle is that rescue depends on being found, which requires active signaling rather than passive waiting.
exam_tip: Signaling methods: visual (mirrors, bright colors, smoke), audible (three blasts, whistles), radio (ELT, survival radio). Use multiple methods simultaneously.
key_facts: Visual signaling: bright colors, mirrors, smoke fires
Audible signaling: three-blast patterns, whistles
Radio signaling: ELT and survival radios most reliable
Ground-to-air signals: SOS, X patterns visible from aircraft
